text: CARIACICA, or CARIJACICA, a parish and small port of Brazil, in the prov. of Espiritu-Santo, on the N side of the bay of that name, 2 m. NNE of Victoria, and 10 m. ENE of Vianna.

CARIACO, a town and port of Venezuela, in the prov. and 40 m. E of Cumana, on a river and at its junction with the large gulf of the same name. Pop. 7,000. It possesses an active commerce. The locality, although insalubrious, is fertile, producing cotton, cacao, and sugar.—The gulf, the centre of which lies in N lat. 10° 30', and W long. 64° 5', is 39 m. in length, and from 5 to 8 m. in breadth. It is enclosed on all sides except the SW by lofty mountains, and is consequently well-watered, and has good anchorage in every part, with clean shores, except in the vicinity of Cumana. It is supposed to have a subterranean communication with the volcano of Cumucuta. The town of Cumana stands at the S side of its entrance. The river C. descends in several streamlets from the Santa Bergantin, and has a total course of about 24 m.

CARIA'II, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, district and 18 m. ESE of Rossano, and 42 m. ENE of Cosenza, on a promontory bathed by the Ionian sea. Pop. 2,144. It is defended by ancient fortifications; and contains a cathedral, 4 churches, a diocesan seminary, a custom-house, and an alms-house. Silk is extensively cultivated; and the manna produced in the environs is esteemed the best in Calabria. The coast abounds with fish. This town, the ancient Paternum, was formerly one of considerable importance.
CARIBANA POINT, a headland of New Grenada, in the prov. and at the E side of the entrance of the bay of Choco, in N lat. 8° 38'.

CARIBBEAN ISLANDS, a name sometimes given to such of the West India islands as lie in a line from Anguilla on the N, to Tobago on the S; and form the E boundary of the Caribbean sea. The name has been loosely applied to the whole of the West India islands; but is more particularly understood of that archipelago which lies between 58° and 63° W long., and 11° and 19° N lat. The principal are St. Christopher's, Guadaloupe, Antigua, Montserrat, Mariegalante, in the Leeward islands; Dominica, Martinico, St. Lucien, and St. Vincent's, in the Windward islands; and Grenada, Tobago, and Barbadoes.

CARIBBEAN SEA, that part of the Atlantic ocean, which has on the N the islands of Jamaica, St. Domingo, Porto Rico, and the Virgin islands; on the E the Caribbean islands; on the S Colombia; and on the W Guatimala. It is navigated by tradingvessels from all the ports of Europe, and is remarkable on account of several phenomena. The first of these is the effect of a gentle motion impressed on the ocean by the equatorial currents from E to W, towards the American continent, through the openings in the chain of the Lesser Antilles. This uniform movement is not accompanied with much danger to navigation. The ocean in these latitudes is so calm, and so seldom subject to storms, that the Spaniards have given it the name of 'the Ladies' sea.' It must not, however, be imagined, that the current is less powerful because the waters are not agitated; for the course of vessels is sensibly accelerated by this current between the Canaries and South America; and a direct passage from Carthagena to Cumana, and from Trinidad to Cayenne, is rendered impracticable by it.—The general stillness of this sea is also occasionally disturbed by violent hurricanes and tempestuous gusts, which rush through the narrow openings in the chain of the Antilles. But in fine weather, its waters are so transparent, that the mariner can discern fish and coral at 60 fath. below the surface; the ships seem to float in the air; and the spectator is often seized with vertigo, while he beholds through the crystalline fluid submarine groves or beautiful shells glittering among tufts of fuci and sea-weed. [M. Brun.] See Antilles. For the following useful account of the English mail-packet system pursued on this sea, and along the adjacent coasts, we are indebted to the columns of that able journal, the Daily News. "The West India packet service is the first attempt to carry out the English post-office system on a gigantic scale on the high seas, and was intended to give the inhabitants of the islands and seaports that stud and bound the Caribbean sea a frequent and rapid interchange of communication one with another, and with Great Britain, on the plan adopted with the provincial towns and metropolis of England. The whole of the correspondence conveyed by the West India packets is collected in London, and despatched across the Atlantic to 34 places; and from these places the inhabitants of the Antilles, Mexico, the Spanish main, and Central and Western America, derive their correspondence. They receive return correspondence from each other and from England, according to their locality, at intervals varying from a few days to 3 months. Eleven vessels are employed in conveying this correspondence, and which are constantly running at the rate of 7 and 8 m. an hour, connecting ports 4,000 m. distant from each other, performing during the year an aggregate distance of more than 500,000 m., and distributing during that period nearly 30,000 letter-bags. To superintend the landing and embarking of these bags, a lieutenant of the royal navy is constantly on board each vessel. The steamers that perform this service, and the routes they run over, are numbered from 1 to 8. No. 1 runs between Southampton and Tampico, touching at Bermuda, Nassau, Havannah, Mobile, and Vera Cruz, and returns by the same route. No. 2 runs between Southampton and Havannah, touching at Madeira, Barbadoes, St. Thomas, Jacmel, Jamaica, and Santiago, and returns by the same route, with the exception that it comes home direct from St. Thomas, via the Azores. No. 3 runs between Bermuda and Porto Rico; No. 4 between Barbadoes and Demerara; No. 5 between St. Thomas and Venezuela; No. 7 between Jamaica and Colombia, New Grenada, and the Mosquito territory; and No. 8 between Havannah and Honduras. The routes performed by Nos. 1 and 2 may be considered the trunk lines of two ocean-railways, each nearly 6,000 m. long; and Nos. 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, feeders or branch-lines, each one varying from 500 to 1,600 m. in length. By looking at a map, it will be perceived that every one of the Antilles, and every port on the Spanish main and in the Mexican gulf, is connected by a chain of steam-communication one with another and with this country. The great art and difficulty in planning the routes, consisted in timing the departure and arrival of the vessels, so as to allow the inhabitants of each place as much time as possible to answer correspondence, and to prevent one vessel from being kept waiting for another. The steamer that leaves South- * CAR ampton on the 17th of the month, arrives at Barbadoes and exchanges mails with Nos. 4 and 5. She now moves on to Havannah, touching the northern islands, where she exchanges bags from, for those for, England, and transfers mails to steamers Nos. 3, 6, and 7, which are at St. Thomas and Jamaica to receive them, thus enabling the southern islands to correspond with the northern. At Havannah she meets the homeward and outward Gulfsteamers, one of which left Southampton a fortnight before she left, and the other a fortnight after. She transfers to the homeward one the colonial mails for England, and receives the Gulf mails for the colonies. To the outward one she transfers the colonial mails for Mexico, and receives the mails from England for the Greater Antilles. She now returns homeward, collecting mails from the Greater and Lesser Antilles and the Spanish main, and enables the northern islands to correspond with the southern." CARIBBEES, CARAIBS, CARIBES, or CHARIBBS, the original inhabitants of the Caribbean islands, who, in consequence of domestic broils, emigrated from N America, in the neighbourhood of Florida, to these islands, and to Guayana in S America. They often engaged in wars against the European colonists; but were almost entirely expelled from the West Indian islands in the 18th cent. They were distinguished from the effeminate tribes which inhabited Hispaniola, and several of the other islands, by their fierce and warlike spirit. Their manners were barbarous in the extreme; and they carried on their wars in a spirit of unrelenting cruelty. Hunting and war formed their sole employments; and their whole ingenuity was exerted to attain perfection in these pursuits. The villages in which they resided resembled a European encampment; their cabins being built of poles fixed circularly in the ground, drawn to a point at the top, and covered with the leaves of the palm tree. Their arts and manufactures displayed a degree of ingenuity scarcely to be expected amongst a people so little removed from a state of nature as to reject all dress as superfluous. A substantial sort of cotton-cloth was observed by Columbus in all the islands which he visited, and the natives possessed the art of staining it with various colours; though the C. delighted chiefly in red. Their religion resembled that of almost all savage tribes, consisting in the belief of a deity, and of a future state of rewards and punishments, together with an ample superstructure of fable reared upon this foundation of natural truth. They believed in the influence of demons and evil spirits, and offered to them, by the hands of their magicians, sacrifices and worship. They had also many superstitious and degrading customs. They are identical in outward appearance with the African Negro. The last remnants of this race in the West Indies were transported from St. Vincent, in 1795, to the island of Ruatan on the E coast of Central America, whence they have spread over the coast of the mainland near Truxillo.

CARIBOU, an island of Lower Canada, in the St. Lawrence, in W long. 67°.—Also an island of Upper Canada, in Lake Superior, 23 m. S of the island of Michipicoten, in N lat. 47° 23', W long. 86° 5'. It is a low woody isle, 13 m. in diam.
CARICHANO, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of Guayana, on the r. bank of the Orinoco, and 10 m. NE of the confluence of the Mita.
CARICHAPO, an affluent of the Juruario, in Venezuela, in the prov. of Guayana, 40 m. NW of the junction of that river with the Cayuni.
CARIEY, a tribe of aborigines in New Grenada, who inhabit the banks of the Apapuris, Piraparana, and Taraira, between 0° and 1° S lat

CARIFE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Ultra, district and 11 m. SSE of Ariano. Pop. 2,558. It contains a collegiate church, a convent, an hospital, a school, and an alms-house. It occupies the site of the ancient Callifæ.

CARIGNAN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Sedan. The cant. comprises 25 com., and in 1831 contained 10,529 inhabitants.—The town is 12 m. ESE of Sedan, on the r. bank of the Chiers. Pop. 1,382. It contains a spinning-mill, a manufactory of white iron, grain and fulling-mills, tile and brick-works, saw-mills, &c., and has a considerable commerce in grain and woollen fabrics.

CARIGNANO, or Carignan, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandimento, in the prov. and 12 m. S of Turin, and 5 m. NW of Carmagnola, on the l. bank of the Po, which is here crossed by a timber bridge. It is surrounded by old walls, contains a fine square, a handsome church, 4 convents, a college, and 2 hospitals; and has manufactories of silk, comfits, &c., and sugar-refineries. In 1544, it was taken and its fortifications destroyed by the French. It gives its name to the now reigning branch of the house of Savoy.
CARIGUANOS, a district of Brazilian Guayana, to the S of the Santa-de-Tumucuraque, inhabited by aborigines of the same name.

CARIMATA, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Carimata, or Billiton Passage, in S lat. 1° 40', E long. 108° 55'. It is about 30 m. in circumference, and rises in the centre in a lofty and generally cloud-capped peak. It is to a great extent covered with wood, and is inhabited.—The C. Passage is an extensive channel stretching between the SW coast of Borneo and the island of Billiton.
CARIMBATAY, a town of Paraguay, on an affluent of the Xexui, 150 m. NE of Asuncion.
CARIMI, a small river of British Guayana, which unites with the Cayuni, 40 m. W of the junction of that river with the Essequibo.

CARIMON ISLANDS, two islands in the strait of Malacca, in N lat. 1° 5', E long. 103° 25'. The larger is 14 m. in length and from 3 to 6 m. in breadth, and, with the exception of a ridge of mountains on its N border, is low and swampy. The smaller is rocky, with a hard clayey soil. Both are covered with wood, and are still uninhabited. On the NE coast of the larger is a harbour, which is occasionally visited by vessels from the adjacent coasts.

CARIMON JAVA, a group of islets in the Asiatic archipelago, in the Java sea, in S lat. 5° 55', E long. 110° 30'. The principal, of the same name, is about 18 m. in circumference, and lofty in the centre. It abounds with wood, and affords excellent water. On its coast is an establishment belonging to the Dutch.
CARIN, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Dalmatia, circle of Zara, on the Adriatic. Oysters are extensively fished in the locality.

CARINENA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 30 m. SSW of Zaragoza and 15 m. NE of Daroca. Pop. 2,648. It contains a parish church, a convent, an hospital, and an alms-house. The environs are celebrated for their wines.

CARINHENDA, or CARUNHENHA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, in the comarca and on the l. bank of the Rio-de-São-Francisco, near the junction of the river of the same name, and about 75 m. N of Salgado. Pop. 2,000.—The river C. rises in the prov. of Goyas, to the N of the Serra da Tabatinga; runs W, separating the provinces of Minas-Geraes and Bahia; and after a total course of 120 m. falls into the São-Francisco. Its banks are fertile, and abound with deer. They are inhabited by Indians of the Caiapos tribe.

CARINI, a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 12 m. WNW of Palermo, on a small river of the same name, and near its entrance into a gulf of the Mediterranean. Pop. 7,000. It contains an ancient Gothic castle. The environs afford large quantities of manna. On the NW are the ruins of the ancient Hyccara.
CARINIS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the l. bank of the Xingu, near the confluence of the united Guiriri and Carinis.

CARINOLA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavora, district and 23 m. E of Gaeta, on an affluent of the Savone, 15 m. WNW of Capua. Pop. 5,420. It contains a fine cathedral, a collegiate church, a school, and a convent. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Foro Claudio built by the Lombards in 1058. The surrounding district is unhealthy, but is celebrated for its wines. CARINTHIA [GERM. Kärnthen], a duchy of the Austrian empire, constituting the N part of the gov. proceeds the second to the transfer of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contraction of the contract of Laibach, one of the two gov. into which the kingdom of Illyria is administratively divided. On the NE and E it is bounded by Styria; on the S by the Carniolan circles of the gov. of Laibach, by Trieste, and Lombardy; on the W by the Tyrol; and on the NW by Salzburg. It lies between the parallels of 46° 20', and 47° 10', N; and the meridians of 12° and 15° E; and has an area of 4,100 sq. m. [Blumenbach.] It is subdivided into the two circles of Klagenfurt, or Lower C. or Eastern C.; and Villach, or Upper C. or Western C .- The general aspect of this country is woody and mountainous. Like the Tyrol, it consists of a series of mountain - ranges separated by narrow valleys. On the N, it is pervaded by the great Noric chain of the Alps, which runs from the Gross Glockner on the NW extremity of the duchy, -forming a point common to Salzburg, Tyrol, and C.,—eastward to the Gross Predl, a distance of 130 m., whence the Stubalpen or Little Alps run NE towards Bruck. Along this N frontier, the highest summits form the watershed betwixt the basins of the Salzo and the Mur on the N, and that of the Drave on the S. From the Gross Predl, the E boundary of C. runs SSE along the Hoch alp, the Kor alp, and the Schwanberg alp, till it strikes the Drave at the point of its emergence from C. into Styria; from which point it runs SW along the Husva mountains to the Rinka in the Steiner alps; whence the S frontier line runs along the watershed betwixt the Drave and the Save to the Kopiberg; thence SSW to the Luschariberg; and thence WNW along a high mountain-range separating the basin of the Geil on the N from that of the Tagliamento on the S. The W frontier is also mountainous, but presents two passes through which the Drave and the Geil find an entrance into C. from Tyrol.—The principal river of C. is the Drave, which, entering it at a point 12 m. SE of Lienz, flows in a very irregular course from W to E of about 140 m. throughout the entire length of the duchy, emerging from it into Styria at a point 12 m. NE of Windischgratz. On the N or l. bank, the Drave receives, as it advances from W to E, the Moll, the Liser, the Glan, and the Lavant; the general course of these rivers is from N to S. On the S or r. bank, the only considerable stream which the Drave receives is the Geil, which has an E course.—The principal lakes are the Weissensee, the Millstadtsee, the Ossiachersee, and the Klagenfurtersee.—The great roads which pervade the duchy are those which, branching off from the Laibach road, a little to the NW of Krainburg, proceed, the one up the valley of the Save, thence across the N frontier to the valley of the Geil, thence to Villach, and thence W up the valley of the Drave into Tyrol: and the other N by Neumarkel to Klagenfurt, and thence up the valley of the Glan, and the Oleza, to Judenberg in Styria. Another great road pervades the duchy from W to E along the valley of the Drave; and a road leads from Villach SSW to Tarvis, where it bifurcates, the W branch passing by Malborget into the valley of the Tagliamento, and the S branch proceeding into the valley of the Isonzo. The climate of C., though healthy, is cold and changeable, but less so in the circle of Klagenfurt than in that of Villach; that of the elevated districts is very rigorous.—The soil of this province is by no means fertile, except in the valley of the Lavant, and a few other favoured spots; which abound in the fruits of the south. Of 1,676,957 jochs of productive surface, 416,063 jochs in the circle of Klagenfurth, and 461,584 in that of Villach, were under cultivation in 1837; the rest consist of forests, pasturages, and waste lands. Wheat, oats, millet, and buck-wheat are cultivated with success in the plains, while only rye and barley thrive on the mountains; 1 ed, in 1808, to 17,000. They are chiefly located in in general, however, there is not enough of any of these kinds of corn to supply the internal consumption. Some wine is produced near Wolfsberg, and in other parts; but it is of inferior quality, and the total extent of the vineyards-which are confined to Lower C .- does not exceed 300 acres. Hemp and flax are not extensively cultivated; but turnips, potatoes, beet-root, carrots, and other garden-vegetables are raised in considerable quantities. Although the valleys of the Alps furnish excellent pasture, the breeding of cattle is little understood. The quantity of horned cattle in 1834 was 110,346; that of horses, 38,352; that of sheep, 227,671. The culture of bees is much followed in this country; that of silk-worms is but lately introduced. The forests abound in chamois, bears, and other game .- The chief wealth of C. lies in its metallic mines, to which we may add its beautiful white marble and its precious stones. The mining districts are chiefly among the northern Alps; the southern mountainranges, connected with the Carnic and Julian Alps, are mostly of calcareous formation, and comparatively poor in minerals. Silver impregnated with lead is found in many of the mines, and exists in a state of purity at Mieslding and Steinfeld. A productive mine of quicksilver was some years ago discovered near Cappel, the yearly produce of which was calculated at 1,500 or 2,000 cwt. At Fragrant and Kerschdorf, there are copper-mines on a small scale; iron is found in a number of places. But the most celebrated of the C. mines are those of lead, particularly that of Bleiberg, near Villach, the metal furnished by which is the purest in Europe, and is well-known among the dealers by the name of the vellow lead of Villach. The lead mines of Bleiberg yield on an average 16,000 tons of lead annually. C. also produces various kinds of calamine and bismuth; and near Huttenberg antimony is found in conjunction with iron. The largest zinc and calamine works are situated on the Raibl in Upper C. Immense beds of coal are known to exist in C. The principal manufactures of C. are those which have relation to the productions of the mineral kingdom, such as the well-known steel called brescia, iron-plate, fire-arms, white-lead, and sugar-of-lead. A considerable quantity of leather is prepared from goat-skins, particularly from those of the chamois; and cotton and woollen fabrics are manufactured. The principal branch of commerce consists in the exportation of horned cattle to Venice and other Italian markets. See CARNIOLA. The pop. amounted in 1812 to 282,454. The census of that year returned 500 as nobles, 5,000 as citizens, 21,500 as mechanics, and 76,500 under the head of tenants and peasantry. In 1825, the pop. was returned at 287,342; in 1837, at 301,383. Though standing in a more immediate connection with Germany, the Carinthians partake somewhat of the character and manners of the Italians; they are in general gay, frank, and contented, fond of tranquillity, and a simple mode of life. As a counterbalance to these qualities, they indulge in habitual indolence and inactivity, and are, with few exceptions, ignorant, bigotted, and superstitious. Their common food is millet, and their drink little else than water. They speak a dialect which resembles greatly that of the Slavonians of Lower Styria, although a number of them are the descendants of German colonists. In point of religion, the Carinthians are firm adherents to the church of Rome. Previous to the reforms introduced by Joseph II. the number of monastic institutions in C. was very great: they do not now exceed 20. The only sectaries in C. are the Lutherans, whose number amount- $ CAR Upper C. The natural division of C. is into Upper, Middle, and Lower; the political, into the circles of Klagenfurt and Villach, the former of which contained in 1837, 178,523 inhabitants, and the latter 122,860. There are in all 11 towns, 25 market-burghs, and 2,755 villages, with 168 castles and noblemen's seats in the duchy. The principal towns are Klagenfurt, Villach, St. Veit, Volklmarkt, St. André, Gemund, Millstadt, and Feldkirchen.—By charter promulgated in January 1850, C. has its own diet of 30 members which is to meet at Klagenfurt; but its administration is subordinate to the authorities at Laibach. The revenue of this prov. amounted, in 1803, to £260,000 sterling. The valuation for the land-tax in 1837 amounted to 6,332,091 florins, or £633,209 nearly. This duchy forms part of the ancient Moricum. The Carinthians embraced Christianity about the 7th cent., and came under the dominion of the house of Austria on the death of the reigning duke in 1321 It was in former ages customary for a prince of the imperial family to come in person to take possession of the duchy at the beginning of his reign; and the plain is still shown-near the abbey of Marien-saal -where the ceremony took place. In 1809, the whole of the circle of Villach was ceded to France, and incorporated by Bonaparte with the Illyrian provinces; but, in 1814, it was restored to Austria.
CARIOCA, a name given to the aborigines of the prov. of Riode-Janeiro, derived from an ancient fount in the city of that name.

CARIPE, a village of Venezuela, in the prov. of Cumana, in N lat. 10° 11′ 14", E long. of Caraccas 3° 3′ 45", in a little valley formed by the Caripe river in its descent from the heights of Purgatorio and Guacharo, at an alt. of 2,636 ft. above sea-level. The v. consists of some 20 straw huts inhabited by Chaymas Indians, a church, and a ruined convent. The valley produces chamomile, borage, tobacco, coffee, and excellent vegetables. The river C. rises in two head-streams, one of which rises in the cavern of Guacharo, at an elevation of 4,771 ft. above sealevel, and flows through a wooded defile from W to NE, to the Rio San Juan, which falls into the Golfo Triste. The cavern is formed in a rock of secondary limestone, alternating with argillaceous clay, and the Jura or Alpine limestone. It consists of three distinct branches, of which the principal one has been penetrated to a distance of 2,676 ft. It is inhabited by an enormous number of a species of nocturnal bird called guacharoes, from which the Indians prepare a kind of oil which they use to season their food. Humboldt has given a full account of this cavern; and an interesting account of it by Codazzi, translated from the Corea de Cumana, will be found in the Penny Magazine, vol. vi.

CARISBROOKE, a parish in the isle of Wight, co. of Southampton, 13 m. SW of Newport, near the Medina river. It includes the hamlets of Billingham and Barcombe. Area 8,880 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,713; in 1841, 5,613.—The castle of C. occupies the summit of an artificial mount between 50 and 60 ft. high; the outworks are about three quarters of a mile in circumference, and occupy a space of 20 acres. The walls and bastions are still perfect in many places. Within the walls is a well 200 ft. in depth, which produces, in the driest seasons, water of the purest quality. The street of C. is situated upon the banks of a rivulet, immediately under the castle. The interest attached to C. castle, in modern times, arises principally from its having been for thirteen months the prison of Charles I., previous to his trial and subsequent execution.
CARLAT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Vic-sur-Cere, 7 m. SE of Aurillac. Pop. 902.
CARLBURG, or Orozoar, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Wieselburg, 6 m. SSE of Presburg, near the r. bank of the Danube. Pop. 1,750, of whom one-third are Jews.
CARLBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, on the river Glen. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 216.
CARLBOE, a village of Denmark, in the bailiwick of Fredericksburg, 16 m. WNW of Copenhagen.
CARLEBY. See KARLEBY.
CARLENTINI, a town of Sicily, in the Val di Noto, 20 m. NW of Syracuse. Pop. 2,502. It was much injured by an earthquake in 1693.
CARLEPONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Ribecourt. Pop. 1,719.
CARLET, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. SSW of Valencia, in a fertile plain, on the Requena river. Pop. 3,822.

CARLETON, a township in the p. of Drigg, in Cumberland. Pop. 143.—Also a township in the p. of St. Cuthbert, in Cumberland. Pop. 175.—Also a hamlet in Penrith p., in Cumberland. Pop. 81 .- Also a chapelry in Redmarshall p., in Durham, 4 m. NW of Stockton-on-Tees. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 157.—Also a township in Pontefract p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SE by S of Pontefract. Area 620 acres. Pop. 179.—Also a chapelry in Snaith p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 12 m. N by E of Snaith. Area 3,070 acres. Pop. 802.

CARLETON, a town of New Brunswick, in the district and 5 m. SW of St. John, on the bay of Fundy. - Also a township of Lower Canada, in Bonaventure co., extending N from the bay of Chaleurs. It is very mountainous, and to a great extent unfit for cultivation. It contains two villages on the Great and Little Nouvelle, by which the S portion of the town is watered. Pop. 576.
CARLETON-FOREHOE, a parish in Norfolk, 31 m. NNW of Wymondsham. Area 700 acres. Pop. 151.
CARLETON (GREAT and LITTLE), a township in Poulton-le-Fylde p., in Lancashire. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 378. CARLETON ST. PETER, a parish in Norfolk. Area 930 acres. Pop. 96.
CARLETON. See CARLTON.

CARLI, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, 30 m. NW of Punah, celebrated for the cave-temples in its neighbourhood, of which a description will be found in Colonel Fitzclarence's Journal, p. 315; and in Bishop Heber's Indian Journal, chap. 26.

CARLINGFORD, a parish in co. Louth, Leinster. It contains the town of C., and the villages of Rathcor, and Whitestown. Area 20,049 acres. Pop. in 1831, 12,194; in 1841, 12,558. It comprises the greater part of the peninsula lying between Carlingford and Dundalk bays. The C. mountains are a continuation of the Slievegullion range in co. Armagh, and are cut off from identity with it only by the beautiful and romantic vale which takes up to Newry the great north road from Dublin. Their principal summit rises in the immediate vicinity of the town of C. to an alt. of 1,935 ft. above sea-level.—The town of C., formerly a parliamentary borough, stands on the S shore of Carlingford bay, 31 m. NW of Bellagan point, 111 m. ENE of Dundalk. Some trifling quantities of corn and other provisions are exported from C.; and a small retail trade exists for the supply of a district hardly exceeding the limits of the parish. The chief employment is the oyster fishery in the bay. A castle, which probably gave origin to the town, and which still exists as a ruin, is said, CAR though upon doubtful authority, to have been erected in 1210 by King John. The ruin is extensive, massive, and imposing; it is in some places 11 feet thick in the walls; and it crowns, or rather covers, an abrupt rock, one side of which is washed by the sea, while the other overhangs a narrow pass between the town and the C. mountains. Area of the town, 53 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,319; in 1841, 1,110. Houses 230. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 44; in manufactures and trade, 144; in other pursuits, 52.

CARLINGFORD (LOUGH), a spacious bay on the E coast of Ireland, between the co. of Louth and the co. of Down. It enters between Bellagan point on the S, and Cranfield point on the N, and is 1½ m. wide at the entrance; it projects 7½ m., in the direction of NW by W to Warren point, with a mean breadth of nearly 11 m.; and it is prolonged 34 m. NW in the narrow estuary of the Newry river. It is well-sheltered, has good holdingground, and sufficient depth of water for the largest ships, and is the most important marine inlet between Lough Strangford and Wexford harbour; yet it is encumbered with several rocks which greatly diminish its value to the navigator. Its bar has barely 9 ft. over it at low spring ebbs, though it has 17 ft. at high water; and it is subject to heavy gusts from W and NW. A lighthouse on Greenore point, on the W side of the lough, shows a revolving light, of the natural appearance, which attains its greatest magnitude once in every 15 seconds. Another lighthouse on Haulbowline island exhibits two stationary lights, the one above the other, on the same tower. The higher light, 101 ft. above high water, burns throughout the night, and is seen at 4 or 5 leagues' distance, like a star of the first magnitude; the lower light burns from half-tide to half-tide, and is similar in appearance to the higher. A celebrated variety of oyster is taken in great quantities in the bay, and sent to the markets of Dublin, and of many places more distant; it has the peculiarity of green skirts or fins, and presents a very unprepossessing appearance, but possesses a delicious flavour.
CARLINVILLE, a town in the state of Illinois, U. S., 39 m. SSW of Springfield, on the N side of Lake Fork of Macoupin river. Pop. 327. A Presbyterian theological seminary exists here.

CARLISLE, a city, inland port, and market-town, having separate jurisdiction, situated in the ward and county of Cumberland, 301 m. NNW of London; 96 m. S of Edinburgh; and 63 m. W from Newcastle by railway. The ancient city is included between Botchergate and Rickergate and the river Caldew which bounds it on the W; but within these limits is not included the larger part of the buildings and streets which form the city in the more popular acceptation of the term. By the parliamentary boundary act a large district has been added, viz., the townships of Botchergate and Rickergate, and part of the township of Caldewgate. The pop. in 1763 was 4,158; in 1801, 9,521; in 1831, 20,015; in 1841, 23,012.—This very ancient city is pleasantly situated on a gentle eminence at the confluence of the Eden and Caldew. Its three principal streets, which are wide and handsome, diverge from the market-place, and are called English-street, Scotch-street, and Castle-street. At the upper end of English-street, on opposite sides of the street, stand two elegant courthouses, in the Gothic style. Over both the Eden and the Caldew are stone bridges. That over the Eden, consisting of 5 elliptical arches, is connected with the city by an arched causeway nearly a quarter of a mile in length, and was executed at an expense of upwards of £70,000. The county gaol and houseof-correction were completed in 1827, at an expense of £40,000. At the north end of the market-place stands the town-hall, where the mayor's court and the city-sessions are held; a more modern erection, at the east end of the court, contains the councilchamber and other corporation-offices. Of the town, the houses in general are handsome and well-built; the streets wide, carefully paved, and brilliantly lighted with gas. The roads approaching the place are excellent, and the number of houses is rapidly increasing.—The cathedral, standing on the highest ground, in the centre of the ancient city, forms the most prominent object from whatever quarter you tower is 130 ft. in height.—The castle stands at the NW angle of the city. The port of C. nominally extends from the mouth of the Sark to Bankend near Maryport; but large vessels cannot discharge their cargoes nearer than within 12 m. of the city. A canal, however, has been executed between the city and the Solway frith, by which vessels of 80 tons can come up to the W side of the city. This canal is 12 m. in length, and is supplied with water from the Eden, which is pumped into it by a steam-engine, delivering 763,200 cubic ft. every 12 hours. The water is raised a height of about 56 ft. The coastwise tonnage inwards at this port in 1823 was 14,226 tons; and outwards, 7,908; in 1832, inwards, 22,820 tons; outwards, 23,392. The foreign tonnage inwards in 1832 was 2,739 tons; outwards, 741 tons. The manufactures consist principally of cotton goods for the West India market. In 1838 there were 8 cotton-mills here, employing 1,486 hands, and 186 power-looms. The fisheries on the Eden are extensive. The city has a mechanics' institution, a public library, newsrooms, and assemblyrooms.—The Newcastle and C. railway, opened on 18th June 1838, is 63 m. in length, from the Forth in Newcastle-upon-Tyne to the C. canal. The level at the C. terminus is 45 ft. above that at Newcastle. It runs by Haltwhistle and Hexham. In 1837 an act was passed for making a railway from the town and port of Maryport to the borough of C.; and since that period the Lancaster and Carlisle and the Caledonian lines have each assumed C. as one of their principal termini. A most commodious station, with an extensive range of offices and workshops, in connection with the Caledonian and Lancaster and Carlisle lines of railway, has been erected in the centre of the town, and is easily accessible from all points. The station is elegantly designed in the old English style of architecture, and was intended to afford accommodation to the four companies, namely, the Caledonian, the Lancaster and Carlisle, the Newcastle and Carlisle, and the Maryport and Carlisle; but only the two former have availed themselves of it. The income of the city is about £2,000. The assizes for the county are always held here; and the Christmas and summer quarter-sessions. The co. gaol and house-of-correction consist of six wings, radiating from a central building, and containing 130 cells, 13 wards, 26 day and work rooms, and 13 airing-yards. The number of prisoners in 1836 was 459. - The city of C. has returned regularly two members to parliament since the 23° of Edward. The right of election was formerly vested in freemen who had been admitted into any of the eight fraternities or guilds resident, or not resident, whose total number is said to have been about 1,000, but of whom only 280 were resident. The new electors are about 800 in number.—C. gives the title of Earl to a branch of the Howard family. From the comprehensive table of mortality prepared by F. Bailey, Esq., for a committee of the House of Commons which sat in 1827, to consider the laws respecting benefit-societies, it appears that C. is a remarkably healthy place. The following is a statement of the number of persons throughout a term of 10 years alive out of 1,000 born at the several ages and places mentioned: London. Carlisle. Vienna. Berlin. Age. · > = = = = = = = = = = = = = = = = = = Within the last 20 years, the rate of mortality per annum in C. used to be estimated at 1 in 54 of the inhabitants. By a calculation in 1841, however, the mortality was 1 in 39,—a rate actually higher than that of the 15 unhealthiest counties. As the general average standard of mortality throughout England and Wales is 2 per cent. per annum, or 1 in 50, it is evident that C., with its rate of mortality (1 in 39), must have lost its character for salubrity. History.] The origin of this city is by some supposed to have been prior to the Roman invasion; by others it is thought to be of the same antiquity as Severus' wall, and to have been a fortress on that celebrated rampart. In the Itinerary of Antoninus it is called Luguvallio,—a name which Burn supposes to have been formed from the British Llugy da gwal, signifying 'the Army by the wall.' This appellation was by the Saxons contracted into Lu-ell or Lu-all; to which the British Caer, a city, being afterwards prefixed, it became Caer-luell; a term which, by an easy transition, has in more modern times been changed into Carlisle. It was taken by David I. of Scotland, to whom it was afterwards ceded by Stephen. This monarch greatly improved its means of defence, and here he resided for some time after the unfortunate battle of the Standard. Restored to England by Malcolm IV., it suffered a severe siege, in 1173, by William the Lion; it successfully resisted him, but was taken by his successor Alexander. Restored again to England, it was, about 1292, reduced to ashes by fire. To this place Edward I retreated after the battle of Falkirk, and here he assembled a parliament in 1298. Here too he appointed the rendezvous of his forces, destined for the final subjugation of Scotland, and celebrated his birth-day, shortly before he died, on his march against Scotland in 1307. In the long and desperate wars between the two countries that succeeded, C. was repeatedly reduced to great extremity. Here, in 1568, Mary, queen of Scots was placed in confinement; and here, in 1596, 'the bauld Buccleugh' performed the notable exploit of carrying off the celebrated reiver, Kinmont Willie. Standing out for Charles I. against the parliament, it was besieged by General Leslie, and after suffering almost incredible hardships, was obliged to surrender in June 1645. In 1745 it was invested by the young Chevalier, Prince Charles Stuart, and disgracefully surrendered at the first summons.

CARLISLE, a township in Schoharie co., in the state of New York, U. S., 36 m. W by N of Albany. Pop. 1,850.—Also the cap. of Cumberland co., in Pennsylvania, 15 m. W by S of Harrisburg, in N lat. 40° 12′, W long. 77° 10′. Pop. 4,351. The Cumberland valley railroad from Harrisburg to Chambersburg, passes through the town. The general government has barracks and a cavalry school here.— The Methodist seminary, called Dickinson college, was founded here in 1783. It has a president and 7 professors; and a library of 11,000 vols.—Also a town in Kentucky, on a branch of the Licking, 53 m. ENE of Frankfort. Pop. 256.—Also a township in Lorain co., in Ohio, 3 m. S of Elyria, on the W branches of Black river. Pop. 1,094.

CARLISLE BAY, a bay on the W coast of the island of Barbadoes, situated to the S of Bridgetown. -Also a bay of the island of Antigua, 3 m. W of Falmouth.—Also a bay on the S coast of the island of Jamaica.
CARLISLE LAGOON, a bay on the N coast of Egmont island, in the S Pacific, between Howe's point and Portland island.
CARLISLE (NEW), a fishing-town of Lower Canada, in the district of Gaspé, on the bay of Chaleurs, in the township of Cox.
CARLO, a small island in the gulf of Bothnia, off the coast of E Bothnia, about 20 m. in circumf., and intersected by a deep bay on the S and E coasts. It belongs to Russia.

CARLOFORTE, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Iglesias, near the SW coast. Pop. 2,500. It has arates the island of Pago from the mainland; in N lat. 44° 31′ 41″, E long. 15° 4′ 41″. Pop. 900. The harbour was constructed at a great expense by the emperor Joseph II., in 1782. The principal trade is in wood, salt, honey, wax, and fish.

CARLOS (SAN), a small town of Spain, at the S extremity of the prov. of Catalonia, on the Mediterranean, at the mouth of the Ebro, 53 m. SW of Tarragona. A small canal runs from Emposta to this place. - Also a town in Andalusia, on the NW side of the island of Leon, near Cadiz. It was constructed at the expense of government, and contains spacious marine barracks, a naval college, an hospital, and a parish church. Pop. 4,500.

CARLOS (SAN), the capital of the island of Chiloe, in Chile. It is situated on the NE shore of the island, on a fine bay. In 1791 it had a pop. of 1,100; in 1836, of 2,000. The town is "dirty; the streets narrow, unpaved, and crooked; the houses, with few exceptions, are wretched wooden huts, for the most part without windows." [Tchudi.] It has a good port; but owing to the tremendous hurricanes which come on suddenly, and completely hide the land, ships are frequently wrecked among the numerous islands at the entrance. It is seldom visited by European trading-vessels; but boats carry wood, brooms, hams, and potatoes, from it to Valparaiso, Arica, and Callao. - Also a town of Buenos Ayres, on a small river, about 6 m. N of Maldonado.—There are various other settlements of the same name in South America; but they are generally inconsiderable.— Also a town of Venezuela, situated on the river Aguare, 170 m. SW of Caraccas. Pop. 5,000. It was founded by the first missionaries of Venezuela. The wealth of the inhabitants consists chiefly in cattle. The chief products of the district are indigo and coffee. The fruits produced in the surrounding country possess an exquisite flavour, and particularly the oranges, which are found throughout the province.

CARLOS (SAN), a bay on the W coast of Florida, in N lat. 26° 36½, W long. 82° 14'. Several rivers empty themselves into this bay, which affords shelter only to vessels of light draught. The mouth is covered by kays and shoals.
CARLOS DE MONTEREY (SAN). See Mon- TEREY.
CARLOS ISLANDS (St.), a cluster of islands in the Pacific, near the W coast of America; the most considerable is about two leagues in circumference.

CARLOTA, a parish and town of Spain, in Andalusia, 15 m. SSW of Cordova. The town is the second in point of size of the colonies established in the Sierra Morena, by Olivades, intendant-general of Seville, between 1767 and 1769. Pop. of parish 3,200. It was originally peopled by Germans from the Tyrol. It consists principally of isolated cottages, with an air of "German tidiness" about them.
CARLOVASSI, a town on the N coast of the island of Samos.

CARLOW, an inland county of Ireland, nearly in the centre of the southern half of Leinster. It is bounded on the NW by Queen's co.; on the N by Kildare and Wicklow; on the E by Wicklow; on the SE and S by Wexford; and on the W by Kilkenny. Its form—except that a space of about 18 sq. m. is cut out at the NW angle, and an indentation of about 22 sq. m. is made not far from the N end of the E side—is very nearly triangular. Its of plantations, 602 acres of towns, and 505 acres of water. Physical features. The Comer mountains and CAR their eastern offsets occupy about one-half of the small section, measuring 8 by 31 m., which lies W of the Barrow; and the Mount Leinster and Blackstairs mountains, bold, lofty, and of a sable hue, occupy a space of about 16 m. in length, and probably 21 m. in mean breadth, along the SE border. All the remainder of the surface may be regarded as a wing of the great central plain of Ireland, and is either flat or gently undulated. The river Barrow bounds the W side of the county for 51 m.; passes for 8 m. over the neck of a large westerly projection; and then flows 12 m. longer on the W boundary; and, throughout its connection with the county it is navigable, both southward to the Atlantic and northeastward to the Irish sea. The Slaney has a course of about 15 m., chiefly southward, within the interior. The Decreen, an affluent of the Slaney, has most of its course in the interior and on the boundary; and the Burren, an affluent of the Barrow, has the whole of its course in the interior. Geology.] Somewhat upwards of one-half of the county is part of the great granite field of the SE of Ireland. A belt on the extreme E border of the county, above and below Newtownbarry, about 51/4 m. long and 14 m. broad, consists of altered or metamorphic rocks in the vicinity of granite. About four-fifths of the section which lies west of the Barrow, reckoning from the W boundary inward, consist of the coal formation. All the rest of the county, consisting of a considerably broad belt from the extreme N 15 m. down the vale of the Barrow, consists of carboniferous limestone, dark-coloured, close-grained, and well-adapted for the purposes of building. Agriculture.] The soil of the limestone district is gravelly, yet fertile; and of the granite district is variously peaty, gravelly, mixed, and aggregately light. Agricultural improvement is least advanced in the extreme E and W, or in the upland districts; but has been carried to so advanced a state in the limestone region along the Barrow, as to have issued in a decidedly good system of farming. The land is held chiefly in fee; it yields, on the average, 15s. per acre to the proprietor, and 40s. to the occupier; and its aggregate rental is estimated at somewhat upwards of £130,000. Large tracts of rich pasture-land are occupied as dairy farms; and great attention is paid to the quality of cattle, and the improvement of their breed. The total number of farms, in 1841, was 6,296; and of these, 1,933 measured from 1 acre to 5 acres,—2,357, from 5 to 15 acres,—1,056, from 15 to 30 acres,—and 950 above 30 acres. In 1847, the holdings had increased to 7,762 in all. The aggregate value of all live stock in 1841 was £270,334, exclusive of all such stock within the area of the towns. In 1847 the number of horses within the co. was 8,207; asses 1,763; cattle 32,759; sheep 32,043; pigs 16,700; goats 1,716; poultry 91,531.—The extent of plantations, in 1841, comprised 850 acres of oak, 50 of ash, 78 of elm, 26 of beech, 268 of fir, 3,341 of mixed trees, and 313 of orchards,—in all 4,927 acres. The number of detached trees was 560,243,—equivalent in extent to 3,501 acres; and thus the grand total of acres under wood was 8,428. The extent of land under crop in 1847 was as follows: Acres. Acres. $ The produce of crop 1847 consisted of 49,950 quarters wheat; 129,295 quarters oats; 23,839 quarters barley; 9,781 quarters bere; 671 quarters rye; 198 quarters beans; or 34,581 tons of corn in all. Trade.] The staple trade is the raising, manufacture, and exportation of provisions. The landed produce is believed to amount in value to £1,038,000 a-year; and a large proportion of it is sent to the Dublin and the British markets. The corn-mills on the Barrow are, in various instances, the greatest establishments of their class in Ireland, and annually produce, on the average, 350,000 cwts. of flour, and 100,000 cwts. of oatmeal. The quantity of butter annually exported is from 25,000 to 35,000 firkins in the direction of Dublin, and about 10,000 in the direction of Waterford. Much corn is exported; many fine flocks of long-wooled sheep are fattened for the market; and a large quantity of bacon is cured, chiefly for local consumption. The manufacture of coarse woollens was at one time comparatively extensive, but has perished. Communications.] The only water-communication is the navigation of the Barrow. The mail-road from Dublin to Waterford passes through Carlow, down the l. bank of the Barrow. The other roads are numerous, and generally kept in good repair. A railway from Dublin to Kilkenny passes across the W wing of the county, down the r. bank of the Barrow, near Carlow and Leighlin-bridge; and a proposed railway to Kilkenny branches-off from the former in the western vicinity of C., crosses the Barrow at the outskirt of the town, and goes SE to Newtownbarry. A line of railway from Wexford to C., and another from C., via Athy, to Monastereven, have been projected. The length of these two lines would be about 58 m. Divisions and Towns.] The county is divided into the baronies of C. in the NW, Rathvilly in the NE, Forth in the middle of the E, Idrone-East in the centre and slightly in the E, Idrone-West in the W wing, and St. Mullins in the S. The only corporate towns, ancient or modern, are C. and Old Leighlin; the only other place fairly entitled to be called a town is Leighlin-bridge; the principal villages are Bagnalstown, Borris, Tullow, and Hacketstown. The co. is all included in the dio. of Leighlin; constitutes more than half of its area; and contains both its Protestant and its Roman Catholic seat,— the former at Old Leighlin, and the latter at C. The constituency for sending members to parliament amounted, in 1835, to 1,529, and, in 1841, to 1,759. -Pop. in 1831, 81,988; in 1841, 86,228. Inhabited houses in 1841, 14,008. Families, 15,210. Families residing in first-class houses, 532; in second-class houses, 4,531; in third-class houses, 6,050; in fourthclass houses, 4,097. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 10,498; in manufactures and trade, 3,139; in other pursuits, 1,573. Families supported by vested means and by professions, 408; by the directing of labour, 5,363; by their own manual labour, 8,999; by means not specified, 440. Males at and above 5 years of age who could read and write, 16,566; who could read but not write, 7,931; who could neither read nor write, 12,783. Females at and above 5 years of age who could read and write, 10,375; who could read but not write, 12,309; who could neither read nor write, 16,164. Males above 4 years of age attending primary schools, 4,220; attending superior schools, 213. Females above 4 years of age attending primary schools, 3,279; attending superior schools, 423. History.] Long previous to the Anglo-Norman invasion, the territory which now constitutes the county of C. was the scene of innumerable conflicts among the Irish toparchs and kings, and between them and their vigilant and relentless Danish invaders. Strongbow and his little band of resolute followers found the territory constituting the N part of Hy-Kinsellagh, the patrimony of Dermod MacMurrough, king of Leinster, who invited the invasion; and they made it, for a considerable period, the grand arena of their strife with the native Irish,-the battle-field of some of the earliest of those actions which terminated in the conquest of Ireland. William, Earl of Pembroke, married Isabel, daughter of Strongbow, succeeded in her right to the principality of Leinster, granted about the year 1208 the first charter to the inhabitants of C., and, in 1216, got the county erected by the feeble King John into a palatinate in his favour. The Kavanaghs and other native chiefs paid little regard to the political arrangements of the English; and seem to have early reacquired their consequence within the county, and to have obtained ascendancy over it. About 1580, a chief of the Kavanaghs, who had been intrusted with the seneschalship of the county, asserted a right of sovereignty over all the territory which had been ruled by Dermod MacMurrough, and from 1590 till 1601 remained constantly in arms, conducting a scourging system of predatory warfare, but was eventually reduced to submission by Sir Oliver Lambert. During the rebellion of 1798, this co. was the theatre of several actions.

CARLOW, a parish containing a town of the same name, in the above county. Area 3,330 acres. Pop. in 1831, 9,597; in 1841, 9,901.—The post and market-town of C., the capital of the cognominal parish, barony, and county, stands on the E bank of the Barrow, and chiefly on the N bank of the Burren, 53 m. S by W of Castledermot; 71/4 m. WNW of Tullow; 14 m. N of Borris; and 39\frac{1}{4} m. SSW of Dublin. It has a modern and neat appearance. The Roman Catholic parochial chapel—the cathedral of the Roman Catholic dio. of Kildare and Leighlin-is an edifice remarkable for the beauty of its style and the skilfulness of its architecture. It was completed in 1834, at the cost of £18,000.—The Roman Catholic college consists of a centre and two wings, and, though plain in style, is spacious, and has an imposing appearance. This institution was founded by the Rev. Dr. Keefe, and originally intended for the education of youth; it was opened in 1793, under the direction of the late Dean Staunton, and became one-half subordinated to the training of candidates for the Roman Catholic priesthood; and it eventually acquired great fame under the late Dr. Doyle, and became almost a rival of Maynooth. The cost of its erection was about £13,000. The number of its students in 1834 was 107. The routine of tuition comprises Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, French, English, ecclesiastical and civil history, rhetoric, geography, arithmetic, book-keeping, and mathematics.—The new court-house, recently erected on the N outskirt of the town, is an octagonal edifice of hewn stone, with a beautiful Ionic portico on a massive balustraded basement.—The Lunatic asylum for the counties of C., Kildare, Wexford, and Kilkenny, stands on the N outskirt of the town, in a park of about 15 acres. It cost £22,552.—The barrack contains accommodation for a troop of cavalry, and two companies of infantry. The Wellington bridge across the Barrow is an elegant edifice of stone; and the chief bridge across the Burren is a neat iron structure.—The castle of C. stands on a slight eminence overhanging the Barrow, immediately S of the bridge. It was constructed in the Anglo-Norman style of architecture, -a hollow quadrangle, thick in the walls, remarkably low and narrow in the doors, slender even to loop-hole dimensions in the windows, and fortified at each angle by a large round tower.—The C. poor-law union comprehends an area of 179,790 acres, which contained in 1831 a pop. of 74,724. Expenditure in 1846, £3,442.—A considerable trade is carried on by the Barrow navigation downward to Waterford, and upward through the Grand canal to Dublin. A large proportion of the cereal and dairy produce of the county is sold and shipped at C. The manufacture of coarse woollens occupies a few persons. There are 3 breweries, a distillery, and 4 flourmills.—The borough sends one member to the imperial parliament. Constituency in 1835, 474; in 1841, 417. Area, exclusive of Graigue, 445 acres. Pop. in 1831, 9,114; in 1841, 8,734. Houses 1,239. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 438; in manufactures and trade, 808; in other pursuits, 459. CARLOWITZ, KARLOWITZ, or KARLOVICZ, a town or free military commune of Hungary, in Sclavonia, in the gov. and 6 m. SE of Peterwardein, on the r. bank of the Danube, at the foot of and enclosed on three sides by a mountain of the same name. Pop. 5,800. It is the seat of a Greek archbishop, metropolitan of the empire; and contains a Catholic and 3 Greek churches, an Illyrian archiepiscopal lyceum, a Greek theological seminary, and about 850 houses, two-thirds of which are of the meanest description. The streets are partially paved. Its situation subjects it to frequent inundations, but renders its commerce extremely active. The treaty of peace between Austria, Poland, Venice, and the Porte, in 1699, was signed here. The environs are celebrated for their wine.

CARLSBAD, or WARY, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 7 m. ENE of Elbogen, and 24 m. ENE of Egra, situated on the river Eger, and celebrated for its hot springs. This town, which is now much frequented as a watering-place, has a singular position in a deep valley, the sides of which are cut away to make room for the houses, which are built in tiers one above the other. Its alt. above sea-level is 1,300 ft. That part of the town called the Wiese, or 'Meadow,' is the most healthy and agreeable quarter. It consists of a long range of houses, before which is an esplanade adorned with chestnut trees, stretching to the banks of the river Toepel, or Teple. The lower story of the houses is usually occupied by shops and work-rooms. C. is most frequented in the months of June and July. The principal manufactures of the place consist of tin wares and Damascus steel; and the arms fabricated here are known over the whole of Europe. The principal spring, called the Sprudel, discharges with great velocity about 352 cubic ft. of water hourly, through a curious natural vault, or incrustation of stalactite, and has a temp. of 165°. The Neubrunnen, or 'New spring,' is not so warm. Its temp. is 147°. The Muhlbrunnen has a temp. of 138°. The Schlossbrunnen, or 'Spring of the castle,' more recently discovered, contains more fixed air than the rest, and has a temp. from 120° to 125°, but is little used. According to the experiments of Klaproth, 100 cubic inches of the C. water contains,— Grains. Dry carbonated soda, Bromine and potash have been discovered in the water, and some other ingredients. The waters of C. are used in a great variety of disorders, but particularly in dyspeptic complaints, obstructions of the abdominal viscera, diseases of the urinary passages and kidneys, and in the uterine system in females. They are hurtful in acute febrile diseases, or pulmonary complaints. About 5 pints, divided into 14 cups, are drunk at an average by each individual every day. Several hundred pounds of sulphate of soda or C. salts—are annually prepared from the water. The regular pop. of C. is about 3,000; and about 5,000 come annually to it to take the waters. —D. Becher, Abhandlung uber das C., 1789, 3 vols. 8vo.—Carlsbad und die umliegende Gegend von Hubert von Harrer. Prag., 1801, 8vo.—Klaproth, Recherches chymiques sur les eaux de Carlsbad. Berlin, 1770, 8vo. CAR
CARLSBERG, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Rhine, in the district and 6 m. N of Zwey- Brucken, and 3 m. E of Homberg.
CARLSBRUNN, or KARLSBRUNN, a village of Austria, in Moravia, in the gov. of Brunn, and circle of Troppau, on a branch of the Mora. It contains several mineral springs and baths.

CARLSBURG, or KARLSTADT, a fort of Hanover, in the gov. of Stadt, duchy and 35 m. NNW of Bremen, near the confluence of the Gieste with the Weser. CARLSBURG, KAROLY-FEJE'RVA'R, OF BELGRAD, formerly called Weissenburg or Gyula-Fejérvár, a royal town and fortress of Austria, in Transylvania, in the comitat of Unter-Alba or Weissenburg, on the r. bank of the Maros, which is here crossed by a bridge 210 yards in length, and at the junction of the Ompoly, 35 m. NW of Hermanstadt, in N lat. 46° 5′ 21", E long. 23° 25′ 10". Pop. 11,300. It consists of two parts, viz., the citadel from which the place takes its name, situated on a hill, and erected between 1715 and 1735, containing the cathedral of St. Michael, a fine edifice, in which are the tombs of Jean Hunyadi and of several native princes, the Episcopal palace, and the mint. The other part is "a long ill-built straggling village," on the plain. C. possesses a Calvinist, a Lutheran, and two Greek churches, 2 synagogues, a theological lyceum, a Catholic gymnasium, an observatory library, national archives, a royal bank, and an arsenal. It has extensive manufactories of gunpowder, and in the environs is an important entrepot for the produce of the salt-mines of Maros-Porto. C. is the only town in the principality in which the Jews are legally established and permitted to enjoy civil privileges. In 1658 it was ravaged by the Turks.
CARLSDAL, an extensive iron-work in Sweden, in the prefecture and 25 m. WNW of Oerebro, parish and 10 m. NE of Carlskrona, on the Svartelf.

CARLSFELD, a village of Saxony, in the bailiwick and 12 m. SW of Schwartzenburg, and 70 m. SW of Dresden. Pop. 650. It contains some copper works and a glass-house. CARLSGAMMEN, CARLSGAMEN, OF RYBATCHY
CAPE, a peninsula of Russian Lapland, near the S side of the entrance of Varanger-fiord, in N lat. 67° 50', E long. 32° 10'.

CARLSHAMN, or KARLSHAFEN, a town of Sweden, in the laen or prefecture and 32 m. W of Carlskrona, at the entrance of the Mye into the Baltic. Pop. 4,050. It is of considerable strength, is regularly built, and possesses a small but safe port. It contains an hospital and two churches; and has manufactories of sail-cloth, woollen fabrics, and tobacco, and building-docks. Its commerce consists chiefly in grain, iron from the neighbouring mines, timber, potash, rosin, pitch, and tobacco. The adjacent fisheries are extremely productive. Fairs are held twice a-year. C. was founded in 1666. It has been repeatedly devastated by fire.

CARLSHOFF, or OVATICA, an island of the South Pacific, in the Low archipelago, in S lat. 15° 30', E long. 146° 30'. It is about 9 m. in circumference, and is surrounded by coral reefs. In the centre is a large lake. It was discovered in 1722 by the Dutch navigator Roggewein.
CARLSKOGA, a town of Sweden, in the prefecture and 25 m. W of Oerebro, cap. of the herad and jurisdiction of the mines, on Lake Moeker.

CARLSKRONA, a sea-port town of Sweden, in the laen of Blekingen or Carlskrona, in South Gothland, in N lat. 56° 10', E long. 15° 33'. It derives its name from Charles XI., by whom it was founded in 1680, as a station for his ships of war. He removed them from Stockholm to this place, which ~ was greatly preferable both on account of the convenience of its situation and the safety of its harbour; and it has continued ever since to be the grand, or rather the only, depot of the Swedish fleet. -The principal part of the town stands on the small rocky island Trotsö, which communicates with the mainland by a strong dike and a wooden bridge. The mole, too, which lies close to the basin wherein the fleet is moored, is covered with houses; and there are several little islands immediately adjoining, on some of which the more wealthy inhabitants have their country-seats and gardens; on others are erected works of defence for the protection of the harbour. These suburbs are fortified towards the town by a wall. The town itself is extensive and spacious. Most of the houses are built of wood. There are a few tolerable ones of brick. The new town-house is a handsome structure; and the arsenal is a spacious and well-furnished edifice. Of the churches, one or two are rather elegant, particularly the German church, which has a cupola covered with copper.-The harbour of C. is very large and commodious, being capable of holding 100 vessels. It has depth of water sufficient for ships of the largest size carrying their lower-tier guns; and it is perfectly protected both by nature and art from any hostile attack by sea. Before it are many shallows, and nearly 100 inconsiderable islands. Ships of war, indeed, can only enter the harbour between the islands of Aspo and Turko, and these are furnished with batteries which completely command the passage.— The docks give to this town its chief interest and importance. Originally ships, on undergoing repair, were laid on their sides in the open harbour; but in 1714, according to a plan of Polhem—whose mechanical skill is celebrated in Sweden-a dock of considerable dimensions began to be formed by hollowing out the solid rock. This, though not finished till 1724, was found to be too small for men-of-war; and it was afterwards enlarged to the capacity of receiving vessels of the first rate. In length it is 190 Swedish ft., in depth 33 ft., and in breadth 46 ft. When full, it contains 300,000 cubic feet of water; and is emptied by means of chain-pumps. A dock so capacious, and hewn out of the solid rock, was unquestionably a bold and grand undertaking; but it is far surpassed by the new docks, which, in the language of Coxe, "have been begun upon a stupendous plan, worthy of the ancient Romans." The form of the new docks is semicircular. From the centre to the circumf. it is divided into four divisions, each division having separate receptacles for five vessels, a distinct gate 48 ft. in breadth and nearly 30 ft. in height, and a detached edifice over it with a copper roof. The walls, like the bottom, are of hewn granite, about 40 ft. thick, and filled up in the middle with earth; and the roof is supported by rows of granite pillars, which give it a very magnificent appearance. There is one common entrance for the five ships which each of the principal divisions contains. The docks and buildings connected with them are separated from the town by a lofty stone wall, remarkable for having been erected by the Russian prisoners captured by Charles XII. These magnificent plans have not yet been fully executed: only two divisions, in fact, have been covered in. A reservoir with which the docks communicate is filled by sluices opening to the port, and emptied again at pleasure by pumps driven by windmill-sails. Mr. James says that the alleged fact of the gradual subsidence of the Baltic has not been observed here, or has had no sensible effect on the docks. The ships at C. are built chiefly by English workmen. The provinces of Blekingen and Scone not affording a continued supply of oak, this sort of den behind the palace. Many attempts have been ፙኯ CAR timber is partly imported from Germany; but masts, deals, pitch, tar, and the greater part of the flax used in the navy are procured from the Swedish territories. Ropes and sails are manufactured from Riga hemp. Cannon is cast, and gunpowder made with Swedish saltpetre. C. is the place of residence for the governor of Blekingen, and is the tenth in order of those towns which vote in the diet. It is one of the four places in Sweden in which the Jews were permitted, by a decree of the state, to erect synagogues for their peculiar worship.-Its exports consist of tar, pitch, iron, copper, steel, potash, tallow, and marble. According to Diurberg, the number of inhabitants in C. in 1800 was 13,800.—The laen of C. has the laens of Wexer and Calmar on the N; the sound of Calmar on the E; the Baltic on the S; and the laen of Christianstadt on the W. Its area is 25.80 sq. Swedish m., or 1,314 English sq. m., of which the surface of 4-11ths has an elevation exceeding 320 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1751, 35,694; in 1833, 87,700; in 1840, 95,807. Grain is raised in sufficient quantity for the home-consumption; but the principal traffic is created by the productions of its forests, and the rearing of cattle. It is divided into 2 fogderi, 4 herads, and 32 parishes. Its principal towns are C., Ronnely, and Carlshamn. -Coxe's Travels in Poland, Russia, and Sweden, &c., vol. iv.—Catteau, Voyage en Allemagne et en Suede, tom. ii.-Küttner's Travels through Denmark, Sweden, &c., in 1798 and 1799.
CARLSMARKT, or KARLSMARKT, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, regency and 40 m. SE of Breslau, circle and 10 m. E of Brieg, on the r. bank of the Stober. Pop. in 1837, 666.
CARLSOE, an island of Norway, off the N coast of Finmark, in N lat. 70°, E long. 20°.
CARLSOR, an island of Sweden, in the Baltic, near the W coast of Gottland.

CARLSRUHE, or CARLSROUHE, the capital of Baden, 1½ league from the Rhine, founded in 1715 by the margrave Charles William, and now the residence of his successors, who have here a fine palace and delightful gardens. The plan on which the town is built is very regular; and it is also very singular, being in the form of a fan. There is a single street, about 1 m. in length, running in a direction parallel to the front of the palace, and at a considerable distance from it: from this all the other streets, amounting to thirty-two, go off at right angles, and are so arranged as to have their view terminating at one end in the front of the palace, while the other penetrates the forest of the Hart or Haardt by which the town is surrounded on the N and W. The houses, moreover, are constructed with as much regard to uniformity as the streets, being all of equal size and height. The coup d'ail from the top of the palace is extremely beautiful and magnificent. Of churches there are four, two for Protestants and two for Roman Catholics. There is also a Jewish synagogue. Several of the public buildings are by Weinbrenner, a celebrated architect. The literary establishments of C. are chiefly the gymnasium, seminaries for educating village-curates and schoolmasters, an institution for the deaf and dumb, a polytechnic institute, and a library of 90,000 vols. There are some collections of natural and artificial curiosities which deserve notice. They consist of the library belonging to the margrave, his physical cabinet, and his cabinets of medals, models, and of natural history; in which last the collection of tulips is uncommonly fine. Besides these, there is a noble botanical garden; Gmelin's cabinet of natural history; Boeckmann's physical cabinet; and Becker's collection of prints. There is a charming promenade in the garmade to introduce manufactures and industry into C. Several watchmakers from Geneva have been induced to settle in it; and many of the inhabitants, instructed by English mechanics, are engaged in steel manufactures similar to those which are carried on in Birmingham and Sheffield. Meyer's work for grinding and polishing is deemed remarkably curious. The pop. is estimated at 22,000.—[Reichard's Guide des Voyageurs en Europe.—Stolberg's Travels.—Brièfe über Carlsruhe von Brunn.]—Also a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, regency, circle, and 20 m. NNW of Oppeln. Pop. 1,600. It contains a Lutheran church, and a fine castle belonging to the Duke of Wurtemberg, and has a manufactory of potash. CARLSTADT [CROAT. Karlvuec], an administrative division of Hungary, on the Croatian military frontier. Area 107.7 German sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 248,571, composed of the 'regiments' of Likka, Ottoka, Ogulin, and Szluin.-The principal town, of the same name, is well-built, on the river Kulpa, which flows through the town, at the head of the navigation of the Save and the Kulpa, and in the commercial route between the Adriatic and the Black sea. Pop. 6,026. It is the seat of a Greek bishop. The surrounding country yields good wine. There is a splendid road between C. and Fiume.

CARLSTADT, a town of Sweden, in the prov. of Wermeland, on an extensive island called Tingwalla, which is 12 m. in circumference, and is formed by the river Clara, dividing into branches, and afterwards uniting and falling into the Wener lake. It was built by Charles IX. in the year 1584. All the houses, excepting the cathedral and the school, are constructed of painted wood; but the streets are broad and straight; there is a number of spacious buildings; and, upon the whole, it is a pretty place, having the agreeable features of cleanliness and cheerfulness to recommend it to the stranger's eye. It is the see of a bishop, who has here an episcopal palace; and, according to Diurberg, contains 1,800 inhabitants. There is a mineral spring in its neighbourhood. It has manufactories of linen and woollen; imports sugar, tea, and spices; and trades in iron and wood across the Wener.—The laen of C. has Falun on the N; Orebro on the E; the Wener lake and Wenersberg on the S; and Norway on the W. Its area is 157.89 Swedish sq. m., or 6,940 English sq. m. Pop. in 1833, 177,300; in 1840, 195,546. Little corn is grown in the district; the rearing of cattle, and mining form the chief employments. It is divided into five fogderis. Its principal towns are C. and Philipstad.

CARLSTEIN, a town of Austria, in the archduchy of Austria, in the circle of Ob-Mannhartsberg, 18 m. NW of Horn, on the r. bank of the Thaya. It contains a cotton spinning-mill and manufactories of calico and flannel.—Also a village in Bohemia, in the circle of Beraun, and 13 m. SW of Beraun. It contains an extensive fort erected by Charles IV. in 1348, an isolated tower, in the chapel of which the crown insignia were formerly kept, &c., and valuable collections of objects of art.

CARLTON, a township of Orleans co., in the state of New York, 258 m. NW of Albany. It lies on Lake Ontario, and is drained by Oak Orchard creek. The surface is level, and the soil consists of sand and gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 2,275.

CARLTON HOUSE, a provision-post or station of British North America, in the district of and on the Saskatchawan, in N lat. 52° 50′ 47", and W long. 106° 12′ 42″. The district is fertile, and produces wheat, barley, oats, and potatoes. A vast plain commences here, and extends along the S branch of the Saskatchawan, towards the sources of the Missouri. CAR
CARLTON, or BRUE RIVER, a river of Van Diemen's Land, in Richmond co., which runs SSW, and falls into Frederick Henry or North bay, 7 m. SE of Sorell.
CARLUKE, a parish and village in Lanarkshire, on the Clyde. Area of p. 15,360 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,756; in 1841, 4,802. The town of C. is 5 m. W of Lanark. Pop. 2,090.

CARLUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne. arrond. of Sarlat. The cant. comprises 12 com., and in 1831 contained 6,969 inhabitants. The village is on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 8 m. E of Sarlat. Pop. 918.
CARLYLE, a village, cap. of Clinton co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., on the Kaskasia, 215 m. from its mouth, and 96 m. SE of Springfield. It consisted in 1840 of about 40 dwellings.

CARMAGNOLA, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 17 m. SSE of Turin, and near the r. bank of the Po. Pop. 3,500. It was formerly fortified, and has two suburbs. The streets are handsome, and several of them, as well as the principal square, are embellished with porticoes. It contains 5 churches, a communal college, 4 convents, and an hospital, and has a considerable commerce in silk, linen, hemp, grain, and cattle. Markets are held twice a-week, and a fair annually. This town was taken in 1691 by Catinat, and in 1796 by the French.

CARMARTHEN, or CAERMARTHEN [WELSH, Caer Fryddyn, the county-town of Carmarthenshire, in South Wales, situated on the r. bank of the river Towy, 20 m. above its mouth in the Bristol channel, and 17 m. by land from Llanelly, in N lat. 51° 51', W long. 4° 19'. The town commands an extensive and beautiful prospect. The streets are steep and irregular, but the houses are handsome and well-built. The market-house is a good structure, and the town-hall, built of freestone, is adorned in front with Ionic columns. The borough appears to be in a flourishing condition, and the trade is extensive, supplying a large district with almost all imported commodities, and the river being navigable for vessels of 200 tons burthen up to the bridge, which consists of 6 spacious arches. There are no manufactories of any kind in the town. Ships are built here of from 50 to 150 tons; and the shippingtrade has considerably increased. In 1845, 74 sailing vessels under 50 tons, and 54 above 50 tons = total tonnage, 7,305 tons, were registered at this port; and the number of sailing vessels that entered coastwise was <math>1,227 = 40,177</math> tons, besides 43 steamers = 6,506 tons. From the colonies, in the same year, 27 vessels = 3,099 tons, entered inwards, and 35 = 2,192 tons cleared outwards; from foreign ports, 12 British vessels = 820 tons, and 1 foreign vessel = 129 tons, entered inwards, and 44 British = 3,903 tons, and 2 foreign = 257 tons, cleared outwards. C. was, until recently, a creek to Llanelly, but is now a separate and independent customs station. The exports consist of corn, butter, oats, timber, bark, marble, slates, and lead-ore. The imports are chiefly articles for domestic consumption.-C. claims to be a borough by prescription. By the municipal act of 1835 it has been divided into two wards; to each of which 9 councillors have been assigned. C. with Llanelly sends one member to parliament. The municipal and parl. burgh boundaries are co-extensive. Pop. in 1841, 9,403. The number of voters registered, in 1832, was 684; in 1847, 915. The 40s. freeholders in this town vote for Carmarthenshire. The county-gaol and houseof-correction is part of the old castle of C., and very spacious. The hard labour to which the prisoners are subjected is supplied by the tread-wheel.-C. 的复数人名英格兰人名英格兰人名英格兰人名英格兰人名英格兰人姓氏克克斯特的变体 医克里斯氏征 医克里斯氏病 化二甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基甲基 gives the title of Marquess to the Duke of Leeds. The C. poor-law union comprehends 29 parishes, with a pop. returned, in 1831, at 36,968. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district, during the three years preceding the formation of the union, was £14,566. Expenditure, in 1838, £11,440; in 1846, £15,517.—C., the Maridunum of Antoninus, was considered to be the capital of Wales; and upon its erection into a principality, the chancery and exchequer of the southern half were fixed here; and here they continued till the jurisdiction of the Welsh marches was abolished. In 1137, C. was burned to the ground by Owen Gwynedd; and a few years after it was rebuilt by Gilbert, earl of Clare. Of the old town-walls the memorials are slender; and of its princely castle not a vestige remains. In romantic history this town is celebrated as having been the birth-place of the renowned Welsh prophet, Merlin. Three miles distant from the town is a spot called Merlin's cave, where, tradition records, the Lady of the Lake entombed the unhappy magician.

CARMARTHENSHIRE, or CAERMARTHENSHIRE, a county of South Wales; bounded on the N by Cardiganshire; on the E by Brecknockshire and Glamorganshire; on the S by the Bristol channel; and on the W by Pembrokeshire, Glamorganshire, and the sea. Its form is that of a parallelogram, extending from E to W about 45 m.; and from N to S about 25 m. Its contents have been calculated by and Carmarthen, and falls into the English channel 8 m. below Carmarthen, after a course of about 60 m.—The Cothy rises on the N side of the county, and runs chiefly in a SW course for 25 m., till it joins the Towy about 5 m. above Carmarthen.—The Tave or Teify, rises in Pembrokeshire, flows first SW, and soon after S; then turning to the SE passes St. Clear's, and flows into Carmarthen bay, after a course of 30 m. Among the less remarkable streams are the Dulas, the Brane, the Gwendrathyawr, the Cowen, the Towa, and the Amond.—The lakes of this county are not remarkable for extent. Llyn Tegwyn, on the highest elevation of Mynydd-Mawr, or 'the Great mountain,' a few miles to the westward of Llandybie, is plentifully stored with fish.
