Another lake, abounding with trout of a superior quality, and eels of extraordinary size, occurs near the foot of the Carmarthenshire Vann or Beacon, in so elevated a situation that the snow remains undissolved on its shores during seven months of the year. At the base of a considerable hill, near Edwinsford, on the banks of the Cothy, and close to the ruins of Talley abbey, are two other lakes, which communicate with each other so as to present, from some points of view, the appearance of an unbroken sheet of water.—C. may be termed a hilly, rather than a mountainous district. On the N, a chain, connected with Plinlimmon in Cardiganshire, skirts the borders of the county from Brecknockshire to the sea; forming, in the greater part of its range, one side of the vale of the Teify, which it separates from that of the Cothy. On the E, the county is shut in by the long chain of the Mynydd-Du, or the Black mountain, which stretches into Monmouthshire; and in the Vann, or Beacon, attains the height of 2,596 feet above the level of the sea. The only tracts that can be called level, are the vale of Towy, which is about 20 m. in length, by 3 m. in breadth; and the plain of Llaugharne, which has been reclaimed from the sea. II. * CAR Soil and Productions.] A large part of the county is waste, but it is pretty well-wooded; and, with the exception of the mountains, is generally fertile, and the farmers are esteemed the best in Wales. Numerous herds of a small breed of black cattle are reared on the hills, and considerable quantities of barley and oats are grown in the valleys.-The mineral products of the county are iron and lead, coal and limestone. Tinned iron plates, coal, and limestone, are exported from Llanelly, to which there is a railway from Castell-y-Garrig. Coals are also exported from Kidwelly, to which place they are brought from the pits by a small canal, which also unites this town with Llanelly. Woollen stockings are manufactured in various quarters, also leather. Considerable quantities of corn and butter are conveyed to Bristol from Carmarthen and St. Clear's. There are several railroads in different parts to connect the mines in the interior with the coast; there are numerous and excellent roads in all directions; and taking into account the number of stage-waggons and coaches, the district may be considered to possess considerable facilities for the application of commercial industry. General Statistics.] C. is divided into 8 hundreds, which are subdivided into 78 parishes, and 12 chapelries. The hundreds are,- Pop. in 1841. Pop. in 1831. Carnwallon, . . The increase in the pop. between 1831 and 1841 thus appears to have been 5.5 per cent.; while the average increase in all Wales was 13.0 per cent. The pop. in 1801 was 67,317; in 1811, 77,217; in 1821, 90,239.— Of the pop. in 1841, 97,547 were natives of the county.—The expenditure for relief of the poor in 1834 was £33,755; in 1846, £31,298. The rate of expenditure per head to the pop. in 1846 was 5s. 11d.; that on the whole of Wales being 6s. 2d. The annual value of property rated to the poor-rates in 1847 was £174,175.—The total annual value of real property in C. assessed to property and income-tax in 1842-3 was £396,954; in 1815 it was £277,455.— C. sends 2 members to parliament. Electors in 1839-40, 5,535; in 1842-43, 5,630. History.] Of the history of this district, antecedently to the subjugation of S Wales by the Romans, nothing is known, excepting that it was inhabited by the Demetæ, the Difed of British writers. Roman antiquities have been frequently discovered in various parts of the county. The independency of Wales was decided near Llandilovawr in this county, where was fought the last battle for native dominion, between the forces of Edward I. and Llewellyn, prince of Wales.

CARMAUX, or CARMEAUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Monestie, on the L bank of the Cerou. Pop. 1,765. It contains a glass-work and tannery, and in the environs are extensive coal-beds. Copper in small quantities is also found in the locality.

CARMEL, a township of Penobscot co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 65 m. NE of Augusta. It is watered by the Sowadabscook and its tributaries, which afford good water-power. Pop. in 1840, 520.- -Also a township, cap. of Putman co., in the state of New York, 100 m. S of Albany. It presents a hilly surface, and contains several lakes, from which some of the head-streams of the Croton proceed. The soil consists of clay and sandy loam, and is very fertile. Pop. 2,263. The village is pleasantly situated on a lake, and contained in 1840, 250 inhabitants.-Also a town of Eaton co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 868.

CARMEL, a celebrated mountain in Palestine. Though spoken of in general as a single mountain, it is rather in fact a mountainous region, extending from the plain of Esdraelon, in a NW direction, to the SW extremity of the bay of Acre, where it forms one of the most remarkable promontories on the shores of the Mediterranean. The whole may be viewed as a portion of a long mountain-range running as far S as Hebron, but, as it approaches the Mediterranean, presenting a more distinct range of eminences, each of which terminates in a flat summit, sometimes bare and rocky, and sometimes covered with mountainshrubs. [Mission to Jews.] The promontory of C. is, according to Volney, about 2,000 ft. in height; other admeasurements estimate it at only 1,500 ft.; and some reduce it to 1,200 ft. It has the shape of a flattened cone. Its sides are steep and rugged; and its soil is neither deep nor rich. Among the naked rocks, stunted plants, and wild forests which it presents to the eye, there are few traces of that fertility which every reader of Scripture naturally associates with the idea of Mount C.: yet even Volney himself acknowledges that he found among the brambles wild vines and olive-trees, which proved that the hand of industry had once been employed on this ungrateful spot; and of its ancient productiveness there can be no doubt,—the etymology and ordinary application of its name being sufficient evidence of the fact. "No mountain in or around Palestine," says Carne, "retains its ancient beauty so much as C. Two or three villages, and some scattered cottages, are found on it; its groves are few but luxuriant; it is no place for crags, precipices, or rocks of the wild goats; but its surface is covered with a rich and constant verdure." [Letters.] C. is mentioned in profane history. Suetonius tells us, that Vespasian, when he went into Syria to subdue the Jews who had revolted, ascended this mountain to offer a sacrifice to the deity of the place, and to consult the oracle as to his future fortunes. Tacitus, in relating this fact, states that the name of this deity was the same with that of the mountain; that there was no image or statue of him, and no temple erected for his worship; and that there was only one altar, on which sacrifices were offered to him. Jamblichus, however, in his life of Pythagoras, speaks of a temple here, to which that philosopher frequently resorted for the purposes of solitary meditation. In the writings of Scylaxwho, according to Gronovius, flourished in the reign of Darius Hystaspes, and whose geographical notices have been much restored by the labours of the learned Vossius—we read of C. as "the mountain and temple of Jupiter." Phocas, who visited C. in 1185, tells us that there still existed the remains of a large monastery, which time and violence had reduced to ruins; and that a few years before, one Berthold, an aged Calabrian priest, instructed, as he said, by a revelation from the prophet, erected a small temple or chapel, and having collected about ten brothers, took up his abode in that holy place. He was in this situation when Phocas was there. These Carmelites—as they came to be called—in 1205 got a very rigid rule from Albert, patriarch of Jerusalem; which, in 1226, was confirmed by Pope Honorius III., and afterwards mitigated by Innocent IV. In 1229, they left the Holy Land under Alan, the fifth general of the order. Some of them were sent into Cyprus in 1238; some went to Sicily; and some were brought by St. Louis into France. They came into England in the year 1240, and erected a great number of monasteries. The Carmelites have still a convent on Mount C., which is almost wholly indebted to nature for its con- CAR struction and accommodations. It has a small chapel containing two altars, one of which is consecrated to the Virgin Mary, and the other to Elijah. It was destroyed in 1821, but has been rebuilt, and is at this moment "the largest and richest in Palestine." [Nugent. The alt. of the convent above sea-level is 600 ft. Behind it, on the SE, the wooded crest of the mountain gradually rises to above 1,000 ft. Mariti tells us that he saw the vestiges of many convents, and some most beautiful columns of oriental granite on Mount C. The Arabs did much to destroy the edifices on this mount, the materials of which they transported to Damietta, where there were no stones fit for building. On one part of the mountain there is a field called 'the Field of cucumbers,' because it contains a great many round stones, the inside of which, being a sparry substance, resembles the pulp of that fruit. Djezzar, the pasha of Acre, told Dr. Clarke that on this mountain "he had found several thousand large balls, and never could discover a cannon to fit them." Dr. Clarke supposed, that by these balls the pasha meant these mineral concretions. Besides some rare plants, Mount C. produces sage, rue, wormwood, hyssop, lavender, and parsley; also hyacinths, anemones, tulips, and ranunculuses.-Relandi Palæstina Illustrata.-Egmont and Heywood's Travels, vol. i.—Mariti's Travels through Cyprus, Syria, and Palestine, vol. ii. - Carne's Letters. - Tischendorff's Travels.

CARMEN, a village of Mexico, in the prov. and 120 m. NW of Chihuahua.—Also a town of La Plata, in the prov. of Buenos Ayres, on the N bank of the Cusu Leubu or Rio Negro, 15 m. from its embouchure. It forms a kind of convict settlement for Buenos Ayres.
CARMENA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. WNW of Toledo. Pop. 2,600.
CARMEN (EL), a town of New Grenada, in the prov. of Antioquia, in N lat. 6° 12'. Pop. 1,200.

CARMEN ISLAND, an island of Yucatan, in the gulf of Mexico, in the bay of Campeachy, stretching along the N side of the Lake Terminos for about 20 m. Its cap., Laguna, forms the great logwood depot for the district.—Also an island in the gulf of California, opposite Loretto, in N lat. 26° 15'. It is 33 m. in length, and 9 m. in average breadth. It has a large salt lake, which has a solid surface of several ft. thickness of salt. The salt is exported to Mazellan and San Blas.
CARMI, a village, cap. of White co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., on the Little Wabash, 181 m. SE of Springfield. Pop. in 1840, 350.
CARMICHAEL, a parish in the upper ward of Lanarkshire, stretching along the Clyde. Area 11,500 acres. Pop. 874.

CARMIGNANO, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. and 12 m. W of Florence. Pop. 1,389. The environs are celebrated for their wine known as Montabiolo, and in the vicinity is the fine grand ducal villa of Poggio-a-Cajano.

CARMO, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, and comarca of Palma, between the confluence of the Matança and Sucuriú, 45 m. NNE of Natividade, in N lat. 10° 46'. It contains gold-mines, but agriculture forms the chief employment of its inhabitants. -Also a town in the prov. of Goyaz, 8 m. N of Sao Feliz.-Also a river in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, which, after a circuitous course of about 50 m., in which it passes Marianna and Ouro-Preto, unites with the Guallacho on the r. bank, forming one of the principal head-streams of the Doce.

CARMONA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 20 m. NE of Seville, on the road to Ecija, on a hill surrounded by fertile plains, and near the l. bank of the Corbones, in N lat. 37° 28' 1", W long. 4° 38'. Pop. 13,072. Its streets are wide, it is proposed to extend a mile out into the river. clean, and well-paved; and it contains 7 parishchurches, and several convents and hospitals. C. is an ancient town, and is mentioned by Julius Cæsar as a strong point. "Its position, considered relatively with the adjacent ground, is indeed most commanding; being on the edge of a vast plateau of very elevated land, which, stretching many miles to the S, falls abruptly along the course of the river Corbones." [Scott.] It is called indifferently by ancient writers Carmo and Carmona; and contains many relics of its ancient origin—of these the gate of Cordova is a magnificent Roman arch, and the ruins of the Moorish castle are still to be found scattered over an extensive area. Woollen fabrics, linen, hats, leather, soap, and wax-candles are extensively manufactured in the locality, and the environs produce large quantities of oil and wine. A fair is held once a-year. This town is of great antiquity, and was granted the privileges of a city by Cæsar.
CARMUNNOCK, a parish and village in the lower ward of Lanarkshire. Area of p. 3,000 acres. Pop. 717. The v., 3½ m. from Rutherglen, has a pop. of 390.
CARMYLE, a village in the p. of Old Monkland, in Lanarkshire, near the Clyde. Pop. 238.
CARMYLIE, a parish in Forfarshire, lying along the Sidlaw range of hills. Pop. 1,107.
CARN, a hamlet in the p. of Dungiven, in co. Londonderry, 6 m. NW of Maghera. Pop. 124.— Also a market-town in co. Donegal, 3 m. S of Malin. Pop. 653.
CARNABY, a parish of the E. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SW of Bridlington. Area 1,950 acres. Pop. in 1841, 185.

CARNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Quiberon, on a height near the coast, 20 m. SE of Lorient, and 18 m. WSW of Vannes. Pop. in 1841, 3,437. In an adjacent plain is a remarkable druidical monument consisting of upwards of 4,000 stones from 3 to 7 metres in height, pinnacle-shaped but pointing downwards, and standing at equal distances. They form 11 ranges, and are supposed to have originally covered an area of upwards of 9 m.
CARNAC. See KARNAC.
CARNAGH, a parish in co. Wexford, <math>3\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SE by S of New Ross. Area 2,106 acres. Pop. 352.
CARNALESE, a village of Nice, 1 m. W of Monaco, near the coast, in which is a castle belonging to the princes of Monaco.
CARNALWAY, a parish in co. Kildare, on the N bank of the Liffey. Area 3,840 acres. Pop. 1,181. CARNAM, CARNOM, or Ko-Sama, an island of the gulf of Siam, in N lat. 9° 55', and E long. 100° 5'.

CARNARVON, or CAERNARVON, a borough and town in the parish of Llanbeblig, hund. of Is-Gorfai, co. of Carnarvon; 235 m. NW of London; on the E side of the Menai strait, which is here 2 m. broad; and at the mouth of the river Seiont. The town is not large, nor of much importance; but many new houses have been erected of late years. Within the walls the town consists of ten small streets, of which the principal is High street. Without the walls, it has double the number of streets, with several handsome villas in the suburbs. The new baths deserve to be noticed, as uniting elegance and utility; they consist of two suites of hot and cold sea-water baths, with a large swimming-bath and dressing-rooms attached to each. The water is drawn from the sea, at the distance of half-a-mile, by a steam-engine, through iron pipes, and received into large iron reservoirs. They were erected by the Marquess of Anglesey, and cost upwards of £10,000. At the N end of the terrace, without the wall, is a pier which | English markets. The cattle are smaller than those CAR At the S end of the terrace stands the custom-house, from which the quay extends along the bank of the river under the walls of the castle. The harbour has been of late enlarged and improved. The port is frequented by vessels of from 50 to 400 tons burden. The principal exports are slate and copperore. The former are brought from the quarries of Llandberris and Llanllffin, a distance of 10 m., by a railroad of recent construction; and are sent to all parts of Europe and America. The coasting-trade of this port is carried on with London, Dublin, Bristol, Glasgow, Cork, Waterford, and Liverpool, to which latter port there is steam conveyance. The imports are colonial produce, Manchester and Birmingham goods, groceries, London porter, &c .- C. received from Edward I. the first royal charter granted in the principality. In conjunction with Crickieth, Pwllheli, Nevin, Conway, and Bangor, it sends one member to parliament. Electors in 1847, 427.—Edward II. was born here, in the castle built by his father, Edward I., one of the finest structures of the kind in Europe, and externally still entire. It occupies a strong position at the W end of the town, and, before the use of artillery, must have been impregnable. - Pop. including the parish of Llanbeblig, in 1801, 3,626; in 1831, 7,642, of whom 6,877 were in the borough; in 1841, 7,843.

CARNARVONSHIRE, or CAERNARVONSHIRE, a county of N Wales; bounded on the W and N by Carnarvon bay, and the Menai strait; on the E by Denbighshire; on the SE by Merionethshire; and on the S by Cardigan bay. It extends from N to S about 48 m.; from E to W about 20 m.; and has been calculated to contain 348,160 statute acres, scarcely one-half of which is fit for cultivation. Physical features.] This is the most elevated county of Wales, and its mountain-ranges are among the most lofty in Britain. Here, wreathing his head in clouds, rises the celebrated Y-Wyddffa, or Snowdon, 3,557 feet above the level of the sea; and here— though shorn of half their terrors by an excellent turnpike road, which carries the traveller round their base with ease and safety-still frown "the cliffs of Penmaen Mawr, heaped hideous to the sky." Numerous lakes glitter among the mountains, and innumerable rivulets brawl around their bases, bearing beauty and fertility into the long and narrow valleys, where the simple inhabitants cherish the customs, and preserve in a high degree the simple manners of their ancestors.—The principal river is the Conway or Conwy, the Toisobius of Ptolemy, which rises in Llyn Conwy, and flows into the Irish sea after a course of 30 m., for 12 of which it is navigable. Its tributaries are the Machno, the Lleder, and the Llugwy.—The Glas Llyn, a romantic stream, discharges itself into Cardigan bay after a course of about 16 m.—The Seiont and the Gwrfai both descend from the sides of Snowdon, and flow NW into the Menai.—The mountains, composed of clay-slate of sundry formation, in many places contain copper, and in others lead; they also afford pure slate of the finest quality, great quantities of which are exported. The vale of Conway, in which the river of that name runs along the whole E border of the county, is a long and narrow tract, equally romantic and beautiful. It affords rich pasturage, and a succession of corn-fields and groves; and forms a pleasing contrast to the bleak region of Snowdon frowning above it. The agriculture is necessarily of the pastoral kind: dairy-farming and cattle, horse and sheepbreeding, are the principal occupations of the farmer. Where corn is grown, the principal crops are barley and oats. Black cattle are reared on the hills for of Anglesey; the sheep also are a very diminutive breed, not unlike Merinoes. Goats are reared, but not in such numbers as formerly; the pigs resemble those of Ireland, being tall and meagre. The farmers are chiefly dairymen, making their rents from the sale of butter, wool, and lambs. It would be unjust not to observe that agriculture here, formerly at so low an ebb, has been much improved; and that consequently the quantity of produce has been augmented. The employment of the poorer inhabitants, in summer and winter, after the necessary attention to their cattle and domestic concerns, is carding and spinning the wool of their own flocks, from which they manufacture large quantities of cloth and stockings. By the erection of the bridge over the Menai this county has been connected with road through the wild and untrodden tracts of C. valueless.—Railroads have been introduced at Llanberris, Llandegai, Penrhyn, and Carnarvon; and have opened a wide field for profitably employing the labour of the poor, and the increasing capital of the rich. The total length of the public roads within the co. in 1839 was 864 m., of which 158 were turnpikes. This county is divided into General statistics. 11 hundreds, viz.: Pop. in 1841. Pop. in 1831. The pop. thus appears to have increased at the rate of 22 per cent. betwixt 1831 and 1841; the average increase on all Wales being 13 per cent. The pop. in 1801 was 41,521; in 1811, 49,336; in 1821, 57,958. Of the pop. in 1841, 71,223 were natives of the co.—The expenditure for relief of the poor in 1834 was £20,136; in 1846, £24,820. The rate of expenditure per head to pop. in 1846 was 6s. 1d.; that for all Wales being 6s. 2d.—The county returns a member to parliament. The number of electors in 1842-3 was 2,353.—The annual value of real property in the co. assessed to the property tax in 1842-3 was £251,043. The annual value of assessed property in 1815 was £125,198. History.] C., in common with four of the neighbouring counties, was called Gwynedd in early times. In the successive and protracted endeavours of Romans, Saxons, Normans, and English, for the subjugation of Wales, this county was the scene of continued and desperate warfare. Among its antiquities may be mentioned the Welsh castles of Dolwydellan, Crickieth, and Dolbadern, and the stately structures of Carnarvon and Conway castles, build by Edward I. Numerous Roman antiquities have at different times been discovered in this county.

CARNATIC, a province of Hindostan, on the E side of the peninsula, which anciently comprised all that part of it that lies S of the Gundigama and Tungabudra rivers, from the coast of Coromandel on the east, to the Ghaut mountains on the west; and was divided into Balla-ghaut and Payen-ghaut, or the Upper and Lower ghauts,—the former being the W part, and the latter the E part, or the Carnatic according to its present definition. It extends from the parallel of 8° to that of 16° N, and between the meridians of 77° and 81°; and is bounded on the N by the N Circars, from which it is separated by the Gundigama river; on the E by the bay of Bengal, along which its coast extends 650 m.; on the S it has the gulf of Manaar and the Indian ocean; on the W it has Travancore, Malabar, the Mysore, and the ceded districts. Its length is about 550 m.; its average breadth 75 m. It is divided, as we have stated, by the chain of hills called the Eastern ghauts, into an E and W section; and is further divided from N to S into three districts: viz., the Northern C., the Central C., and the Southern C. Its districts are as follows: Pop. in 1836-7. Area. 12,000 sq. m. General aspect.] The C., in general, is level in surface, and presents a sameness of appearance, of which those who have only had an opportunity of traversing mountainous regions can scarcely form any idea. In the hot season, the dreariness and uniformity of the prospect forcibly strikes the traveller, who has nothing to withdraw his attention from the physical fatigue of his journey but a naked, brown, dusty plain, now and then diversified by small villages or a ridge of detached hills. In the rainy important effects in regard to the nature of the seasons, not in the C. only, but throughout the whole peninsula. The altitude of these mountains is sufficiently great to prevent the great body of clouds from passing over them; and accordingly the alternate NE and SW winds, called the monsoons, occasion a rainy season on one side of the mountains only,—that is on the windward side. It would appear, nevertheless, that a sufficient number of clouds pass over to occasion a rainy season at a considerable distance to leeward, where those clouds descend; although when they pass over the Ghauts they must necessarily be too high and too light to condense and fall in rain there: the ridge of the Ghauts shelters a particular tract only, beyond which the light and elevated clouds that pass over it descend in rain. Madras is within the limit of the sheltered tract, though at least 300 m. to leeward of the Ghauts. [Rennell's Memoir.] The climate of the E division of the C. is considered to be the hottest in India: the therm. sometimes rising to 130° in the shade. The sea-breeze mitigates this intensity of heat on the coast. Rivers.] Though an immense number of rivers intersect India in every direction, yet the C., properly so called, is worse supplied with such as are navigable than almost any country of equal extent. This no doubt operates as a powerful barrier against commerce, and consequently to the progress of manufactures. Little or no internal navigation can be carried on; and the difficulty of transporting goods from one place to another must be severely felt. All the rivers on the coast of Coromandel are subject to sudden swellings occasioned by the rains that fall on the mountains. In the space of twenty-four hours, I it often happens that from being fordable they become almost impassable even by boats, on account of the rapidity of the current. There are only three rivers in the C., properly so called, which it is necessary to mention in this place. The Paliar or Palaur runs in a NE course of about 220 m. past the cities of Vellore, Arcot, Conjeveram, and Sadras. At the last-mentioned place it is extremely broad, and discharges itself into the sea in N lat. 12° 31'. About the middle of October, when the winter commences, and the rain pours down in torrents, this river is sometimes half-a-mile broad, and flows with the utmost violence and impetuosity. The Chonenbar falls into the sea on the S of Pondicherry, after having united, not far from that city, with the Ginji. The Pannar, Ponaur, or Golden river, has its source in the Ghauts, not far from Darampuri in Mysore, runs past Tricalore, and on the N of Cuddalore throws itself into the sea after a SE course of 250 m. Coasts.] It is a singular circumstance that the coast of the C. forms nearly a straight line, and that it has no port for shipping. It is also incommoded with a high and dangerous surf or wave that breaks upon it, and induces the necessity of using the boats of the country to land in. These are of singular construction, being formed without ribs or keel, with flat bottoms, and having their planks sewed together; iron being totally excluded throughout the whole fabric. By this construction they are rendered flexible enough to elude the effects of the violent shocks which they receive from the surf on the beach, which would either overset or break to pieces any boat of European construction. The sides of these boats are raised remarkably high, and are sewed together with the fibres of the cocoa-nut tree, and caulked with the same material. They are remarkably light, and are managed with great dexterity by the natives. They are usually attended by two catamarans or rafts, paddled by one man each; the intention of which is, that should the boat be overset by the violence of the surf, the persons in it may be preserved.—No port for large vessels occurs between Trincomalee on the E side of Ceylon and the Ganges, an extent of 15 degrees. Agriculture.] The progress of agriculture in the C. has been very slow, or it may be said to have been stationary for ages. The soil in many places is fine, and, when the proper quantity of rain falls, requires little labour to produce a most abundant crop; but in many districts it is very indifferent. Rocks or large detached masses of granite are common in the fields, even in those districts that are considered as the most fertile; in other places, there are immense beds of granite, or of that rock decomposed into harsh coarse sand. Under the intense heat of the climate, even the best and most favourable soils require a very great quantity of water. Nature most commonly supplies abundance of this necessary aliment in the rainy seasons; and in that case two crops are produced; but if this be withheld, scarcity, and even famine, is the certain consequence. Throughout the whole of India, tanks or reservoirs of water have been formed. In Bengal, this is generally effected by digging; but in the C., where the features of surface are favourable, they shut up, with an artificial bank, an opening between two natural ridges of ground. Some of these tanks extend from 7 to 8 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth; and are said to be sufficient to supply with water the lands of 32 villages for 18 months, should the rains fail. The water thus collected is generally employed in the dry season, and let out as wanted in small streams, without the aid of machinery. The genial nature of the soil and climate, assisted by moisture, is all that is re- # quisite to produce the most luxuriant crop. When the water of a tank is exhausted before the rice of the fields watered by it is ripe, the crop must either perish, or they must use the yatam or pacota. One acre and nearly one-fifth require the constant labour of 4 men to supply it with water for the cultivation of rice; but the same number of men are able to water three times the same quantity of gardenground, because a smaller supply is required. When there is a necessity for watering by machinery, a deduction from the rent is generally allowed.—The instruments of husbandry are of rude construction; and were it not for the excellence of the soil and climate, they never could dress the ground so as to render it capable of producing what could even support the labourer himself. There are four different harvests in the year. For the first, the seed is sown in May and ripens in September; for the second it is sown in June or July, and cut in October or November. A third seed time is in September, the produce of which is cut in January; and a fourth crop sown between November and January, ripens in April or May. Sugar is cultivated in small quantities. The staple grain of the country is rice. It forms the favourite beverage of the Hindus, whose religion forbids them the use of animal food; and their agriculture is chiefly directed to its production. They bestow also considerable care on the cultivation of maize, the sugar-cane, and cocoa-nut tree. The wheat that is raised, whether from the climate or injudicious treatment, or both, is very inferior. The cotton-tree is widely diffused; and in no part of India does it thrive better than on the dry coast of Coromandel. In the C. the custom is to collect the grain in stacks, or heaps, after it has been thrashed out in the field. To guard against embezzlement, several pieces of clay, stamped with a seal, are then put on the surface of the heap; and, to prevent injury from the weather, it is thatched. The grain continues in these heaps till the cultivator is able to satisfy the renter, either by advancing money, or by dividing the produce. In every village a particular officer, called talliari, keeps watch at night, and is answerable for all that may be stolen. [Buchanan's Travels.]—There are no markets in the C. similar to the fairs of Bengal. The shopkeepers purchase what they have occasion for from the farmers and manufacturers; and retail the articles thus procured in the bazars or the towns. Milk, a favourite beverage, is sold in all its different preparations by women on the road-side.—The animals in the C. are nearly the same as are to be found in the other countries of Hindostan. Roads.] The roads through the C. are in general tolerably good. That from Pondicherry to Madras is excellent. In many places it is bordered by bushy trees, under the shade of which the traveller is protected from the scorching heat of the sun. Every 2 or 3 m. there are chultries, or taverns, in which comfortable accommodation may in general be obtained. These charitable foundations bear a great resemblance to the caravanserais in Turkey and Persia. Manufactures. The manufactures of the C. are chiefly those of cotton, which is wrought into an immense variety of different articles. From the earliest times, the Hindus have excelled in this species of manufacture, and for ages supplied the whole of Europe, a great part of Asia, and the shores of Africa upon the Mediterranean and the Red sea, with cotton goods, whether for the purposes of dress, or of household furniture, &c. Madras and Pondicherry have always taken the lead of all the other towns and cities on the C. coast in regard to commerce. The foreign trade was formerly partitioned principally among the Portuguese, the Dutch, the Danes, the French, and English. What is called the country-trade, is carried on in ships, the property of merchants who reside on the spot. Inhabitants.] The inhabitants of the C., particularly upon the sea-coast, consist of an immense variety of different tribes of men. The city of Madras itself presents in its pop. a strange medley, well calculated to convey a just idea of the variety of inhabitants in the rest. The complexions of the people on the coast of Coromandel are considerably darker than those to the northward; and the native Hindus are generally darker than the Mussulmans who originally came from Tartary and Persia. They all wear white cotton dresses. It is well known, that the Hindus are divided into castes. Four of these are esteemed pure: that of the Brahmans, the Kshatriyas, the Vaishyas, and the Sudra. The divisions of the first class, however, in the C. are different from what they are in Bengal. They are divided into three sects; and though they admit the divine authority of the same puranas or religious books, they interpret them differently, and have each their followers. They consider the second class as having been quite extinct for many centuries. Another very singular difference also exists in this country, no Brahman officiates in any of the temples of the inferior gods, whose altars are stained with blood. The food of the natives is chiefly a vegetable diet and milk. The tari, or fermented juice, and the jagari or inspissated juice of the palmira tree, (Borassus flabelliformis,) are more esteemed in the C. than those of the wild date, to which the Bengalese give the decided preference. They consume great quantities of jagari, which sells in the country for about 10s. per cwt. The tree produces juice at all seasons; and one man can take care of 200 trees, from which he can extract about 482 lbs. of jagari. —A great variety of languages are spoken in the C. The Tamul, the Hindostanee, the Persic, and the Arabic, &c. Many of the names of remarkable places and objects are of Arabic extraction, or at least are to be found in that language. Cities and Towns.] The maritime cities and towns of the C. are Cuddalore, Pondicherry, Portonovo, Sadras, St. Thomas, Madras, and Pullicate, and a few others of lesser note. Cuddalore is naturally a very strong situation, and would certainly have been more commodious for the British settlement than Madras at the time that the French were in possession of Pondicherry. The Dutch formerly possessed the towns of Portonovo, Sadras, and Pullicate. St. Thomas, from 3 to 4 m. to the S of Madras, was formerly a considerable Portuguese settlement. Madras, or Fort St. George, is close on the margin of the sea, and is the principal settlement of the British East India company on the E side of the peninsula. The nominal cap. of the C. is Arcot, the residence, or rather which was formerly the constant residence of the nabob. All the towns throughout the C. are exceedingly exposed to the attacks of cavalry, from the level nature of the country. This rendered it an object of indispensable necessity to erect numerous forts. But from the complete subjugation of the native princes, and the ascendancy of the British arms, it is not considered at present as of any importance to keep these forts in constant repair, excepting those that are on the sea-coast, or command some of the great roads leading to it. Thus the fortress of Arcot is in bad repair; whereas that of Vellore, being a post of great importance, and the most direct route from the country of Mysore, is justly deemed impregnable to an Indian army. It consists of three strong forts, on as many hills. History.] The inhabitants of the C. have laid claim to a considerable share of the fame which they conceive their ancestry to CAR have acquired in the cultivation of learning, and in the opinion of competent judges have made good their title. One thing. however, seems to be placed beyond doubt, that, at a remote period the sovereigns of the C. must have been possessed of great wealth and power, and the country in general must have made extraordinary advances in population, industry, and in the arts, to have left such magnificent monuments of their architectural profusion and skill. The number, variety, extent, and elegance of the pagodas and other public buildings throughout the C. is astonishing: and the duration of those remains of ancient grandeur has been accounted for from the nature of the cement employed in building in India, composed of a mixture of oil and viscous substances, and from the nature of the climate. The Mahommedans first overrun the C. in 1310; but its conquest was not consummated till 1717, when the Nizam obtained independent possession of the peninsula. Mahommed Ali obtained the nabobship of the C. towards the middle of the 18th cent.; and was supported in his government by the East India company on condition of his paying a yearly subsidy of £360,000. In 1801 the C. was transferred to the East India company by the nabob, on condition that he should receive one-fifth of the net revenue of the country.
CARNBEE, a parish in Fifeshire, on the coast of the frith of Forth. Pop. in 1801, 1,083; in 1841, 1,043. Kellielaw in this p. has an alt. of 810 ft. above sea-level.
CARNCASTLE, a parish in co. Antrim, 3 m. NNW of Larne. Area 9,727 acres. Pop. 2,079.
CARNE, or Carna, a parish in co. Kildare, 4 m. SE by S of Kildare. Area 1,457 acres. Pop. 499. -Also a p. in co. Wexford, 3 m. SSE of Broadway. Area 1,963 acres. Pop. 914.
CARNEABA, or Kourou, a town of French Guayana, on the Atlantic, 30 m. NW of Cayenne.
CARNEILLE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Athis, 17 m. NNE of Domfront. Pop. 1,502.
CARNERO, or Noria, a town of La Plata, in the prov. and 20 m. N of Cordova, on an affluent of the Primero.

CARNERO (CAPE), a headland of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Cadiz, at the W side of the entrance of the bay, and 6 m. SW of Gibraltar.— Also a promontory of New Grenada, on the NW side of the gulf of Guayaquil, in S. lat. 2° 20', and 20 m. SE of S. Elena.
CARNERO BAY, an indentation and port of the W coast of Chile, in the prov. of Araucania, to the S of the cape of the same name, and at the mouth of the Quidico.
CARNESVILLE, a village, cap. of Franklin co., in the state of Georgia, U.S., on Stephen's creek, 111 m. N of Milledgeville.
CARNET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of St. James, 13 m. S. of Avranches. Pop. 1,219. It has manufactories of a particular description of linen named St. George.
CARNETIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Claye. Pop. 232. Gypsum is wrought in the environs.

CARNEW, a parish in co. Wexford and co. Wicklow, in the vale of the Derry. Area 23,465 acres. Pop. 7,205. Slievebog in this p. has an alt. of 1,385 ft.—The town of C. is in co. Wicklow, 9 m. W of Gorey. Pop. 979.
CARNEY, a village in the p. of Drumcliffe, in co. Sligo, 6 m. N of Sligo. Pop. 158.
CARNFORTH, a township in the p. of Warton, Lancashire, 6½ m. NE of Lancaster; on the Lancaster and Kendal canal. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. in 1841, 306.
CARN-GIWCH, a parish of Carnarvonshire, 43 m. NW of Pwllheli. Pop. in 1841, 119.

CARNICOBAR, the northernmost of that cluster of islands in the bay of Bengal, called the Nicobars. It is about 40 m. in circumf., of a circular figure, and elevated little more than 6 ft. above the level of the sea. It is thickly covered with wood; but there are several spots upon it well cleared. The trees are chiefly the Barriongtonia, cocoa-nut, Tournefortia, borassus, and areca; the Aletris fragrans, and various other shrubs, are to be met with. Nuts of different kinds, pine-apples, plantains, and limes, are produced in great plenty: there are also wild ginger, yams, and an edible root which the natives call cachu, and which is obviously a species of arum. All these grow abundantly, and with little care; the climate being genial, and the soil rich. The timber affords excellent materials for building and repairing ships, and some of it is of very large dimensions. No grain of any sort is produced on the island. Pigeons, poultry, and pigs are numerous: the latter are of the species called Sus babyrussa. The other quadrupeds are dogs, rats, and a large animal of the lizard kind called by the natives tolongui. Serpents are numerous. The houses or huts of the natives of this island, are generally erected upon the beach, which is sand, intermixed with coral rock; and form villages, each consisting of 15 or 20 houses, and every house containing a family of about 20 persons. They are of a round shape, elevated upon long wooden pillars, covered with thatch, and have no windows: so that they very much resemble beehives. Their entrance is by a trap-door from below; and the inhabitants mount by means of a ladder which is drawn up at night. For the admission of light and air, the flooring is made of thin slips of bamboo laid at certain distances. The inside is finished off with neatness, and is usually decorated with nets, fishing-lances, and other implements of that kind. The inhabitants of C., though of small stature, are well-made, muscular, uncommonly active, and in general healthy. Their complexion is of a copper colour, and their features have a resemblance to those of the Malays, excepting that their noses are not so flat. They have large mouths, and irregular teeth, which the practice of chewing betel renders black and disgusting. The men wear their hair cut short, while the women have their heads shaved bare. Both sexes get their ears pierced when young, and contrive, by squeezing large pieces of wood into the holes, and suspending heavy weights from them, to render them hideously wide and disgusting to a European eye. The men wear a narrow strip of cloth wrapped close and tight about the middle. The women have a short petticoat made of rushes, or dry grass, reaching half-way down the thigh. They speak a mixture of broken English and barbarous Portuguese, which Lord Valentia tells us prevented him from having any difficulty in communicating with them; but their original tongue is that of Pegu, from which the Nicobar islands were peopled.

CARNIERES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 12 m. W of Charleroi, and 14 m. E of Mons, on the r. bank of the Haine. Pop. 1,975. It contains wire-mills and nail-manufactories, the latter of which in 1831 gave employment to 300 persons. Coal is also extensively wrought in the locality.

CARNIOLA, or KRAIN, a duchy of Austria, forming the S part of the gov. of Laibach, in the kingdom of Illyria; and subdivided into the circles of Laibach or Upper C., Neustadtl or Lower C., and Adelsberg or Inner C. It is bounded on the N by Carinthia and Styria; on the E by Croatia; on the S by Istria; and on the W by Trieste. Its greatest length, from NW to SE, is about 120 m.; its greatest breadth, 100 m. Its area is 181.9 German or 3,862 English sq. m .- The face of the country is for the most part mountainous. The summits of several of the mountains, especially in Upper C., are continually covered with snow. One of the loftiest mountains in the Austrian dominions occurs in this quarter, namely CAR the Terklou or Terglou, which, under the parallel of 46° 25', rises to the height of 10,194 ft. above the level of the sea. This is the principal summit in the chain of the Alps which traverses this duchy in a direction from NW to SE; and which, to the W of this central or dividing point, are known by the name of the Carnic Alps; to the E of it, by that of the Julian Alps. Other considerable mountains are the Caleberg, the Leobel, in the chain which separates Carniola from Carinthia, the Rumberg, the Karst, and the Nanas.—The numerous caverns and grottoes with which C. abounds, form a peculiar feature. The principal tract of the grottoes is in one of the most considerable forests in the empire, but marked throughout by the most inhospitable desolation. Some of these grottoes, or subterraneous caverns, are said to be upwards of 4 m. in length. They rise also, in some instances, one tier above another, each grotto extending in breadth and height from 12 to 20 ft., and exhibiting in the interior a variety of stalactites of singular form and appearance. The most noted of these grottoes are the Magdalen grotto near Adelsberg, that of Lueg, that of St. Servio near Trieste, the grotto of Corgnale, and those of the lake of Zirknitz. Amongst these, the grotto of Corgnale is particularly striking, both in respect of the magnificence and the variety of its stalactites, and the length and massiveness of the columns on which the vaulted roof seems to repose. Zirknitz is not more remarkable on account of its grottoes than for other natural phenomena of a very singular kind, particularly its intermittent lake.—The principal rivers of C. are the Save, the Laibach, the Gulk, and the Culpa. Of these the most considerable is the Save, which takes its rise in Upper C., near to the village of Ratschach, and in its course receives the waters of all the other rivers that have been mentioned. This river, though it flows very rapidly, is yet navigable: it discharges itself into the Danube at Belgrade in Servia.—The chief lakes of the duchy are the Feldesser, the Wochein, and the lake of Zirknitz. Both the rivers and the lakes generally abound with fish. The declination of the surface is from NW to SE. Productions.] Mines of iron, lead, copper, quicksilver, and cinnabar, exist in C. The most ancient and the most noted of the mines from which iron is drawn, is situated in the mountain of Eisenberg, or Naseleiro. At Selelniko also, there is an excellent iron-mine, and there is another at Janerburg. The quicksilver mines of Idria, in the circle of Adelsberg, are of great celebrity. They are the richest of this description in Europe; and formerly yielded annually to the amount of 12,000 quintals of pure quicksilver, besides about 6,000 or 7,000 quintals of native cinnabar. Their produce has, however, greatly declined of late years. The C. marble is beautiful, and is found in great abundance and variety in different parts of the country. Alum, nitre, fullers' earth occur; and rock crystals, beautiful hyacinths, eagle-stones, and bloodstones, are occasionally procured.—The vegetable productions of C. are wheat, barley, rye, and various leguminous crops; millet also is a frequent crop, and also buckwheat, especially in the sandy plains. Pears, apples, oranges, citrons, pomegranates, almonds, figs, chestnuts, walnuts, olives, and lemons, are produced; and although C. does not yield a great deal of wine, what is made here, both white and red, is of a superior quality. A great deal of cyder is prepared in the province; and, with a view to this, much attention is paid to the management of the apple-trees; indeed, the fruit-trees in general are well-attended to, and in this department a great number of the inhabitants find employment. Flax is raised, and a little hemp. Artificial meadows are frequent, from which fodder is procured for the maintenance of domestic animals. The ground that is capable of bearing cultivated crops is generally fertile; and, from the management that is adopted, is often made to yield two harvests in the year: buckwheat being commonly sown after the removal from the fields of the other descriptions of grain, and millet in the places which had been previously occupied with flax or hemp. There is abundance of good natural pasturage, by means of which numerous herds of a tolerably good, though not large breed of cattle, are reared. The number of horned animals in the country in 1780 was 44,098; the number of horses at the same period was estimated at 16,232. According to Becker, the number of horses in C. in 1831 was 14,803; of oxen, 48,369; of cows, 65,696; and of sheep, 63,004. The horses of C. are strong and sure-footed, and consequently welladapted for carrying burdens in the mountainous districts. Bees are reared, and likewise silk-worms. Manufactures and commerce.] One great source of the industry of C. is furnished by its mines. There are considerable iron manufactories and wire-drawing mills, and several forges for steel and for nails, established in different parts of C. A great deal of cloth is prepared at Laibach; and woollen hosiery is manufactured in considerable quantity at Weichselburg, at Strachina, and at Newmarktl. The most esteemed linen is made at Bischoflack, in the environs of Laibach, and at Dobronina.—The exports from this province are chiefly iron, steel, mercury, wines, olive-oil, olives, chestnuts, and various other fruits, horses, cattle, linen, woollen stuffs, cheese, leather, honey, ship-timber, with all sorts of little works in wood, such as boxes, dishes, spoons, &c. One of the most material articles of import is corn, of which the native soil does not in ordinary years raise enough to answer to the consumption of the inhabitants. This province is also destitute of fossil salt or salt-springs. Population.] The pop. of C. in 1837 was 436,038. In 1783 it was estimated at 433,000. The peasantry are a robust and hardy race. With the common people, it is usual to go barefooted in winter through the snow with open breasts, and to sleep on a hard bench without bed or bolster. There is a material difference observable between the different parts of this country, in respect of manners, dress, and language. There are two distinct languages spoken in the country,—the Slavonian or Wendish, and the German,—corresponding to the diversity, in point of origin, by which this people are distinguished; the lower classes, it is understood, are chiefly of Slavonic extraction, the nobility of German.-According to the charter promulgated in January 1850, C. has a diet of 32 members, which is to hold its meetings at Laibach. History.] C. was known so early as the 8th cent., by the same name which it still bears. Its more ancient name appears to have been Crania. The Slaves, or Wends, are said to have taken possession of this country after the year 548. In the time of Charlemagne and his posterity it was governed by the dukes of Friuli, and afterwards by those of Carinthia. Under Otto II. it became a distinct margraviate. It finally was vested in the house of Austria; and from a marquisate, which it had long been, it was in 1231 raised to the rank of a duchy. It participated with other provinces in the effect of the division of the Austrian states, from 1395 to 1496. Otherwise it has, with little interruption, ever since its first accession to them, continued to form a part of the Austrian dominions.
CARNLOUGH, a village in the p. of Tickmacrovan, in co. Antrim, 3 m. NNW of Glenarm. Pop. 649.
CARNMONEY, a parish in co. Antrim, 6 m. N of Belfast. Area 8,937 acres. Pop. 6,128.
CARNO, a parish of Montgomeryshire, 10 m. WNW of Newtown, on the Severn. Pop. in 1801, 741; in 1841, 905.
CARNOCK, a parish in Fifeshire, in the Dunferm- CAR line district. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. in 1801, 860; in 1841, 1,270.
CARNOET, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Côtes-du-Nord, cant. of Callac, 24 m. SW of Guingamp. Pop. 1,841. It contains a lead-mine.
CARNOM (Point), a headland of Lower Siam, on the E coast, in N lat. 8° 56', E long. 100° 5'.
CARNOSA, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, on the Adriatic, 4 m. SE of Bari.
CARNOTA (SAN MAMED DE), an ancient town of Galicia, in the prov. and 37 m. from Corunna, at the foot of Mount Pindo. Pop. 1,320, chiefly fishermen.

CARNOUSTIE, a village in the p. of Barrie, Forfarshire, 1½ m. E of Barrie. Pop. 1,268. CARNPRAYAGA, KARNPRAG, or KURNPRAG, a village of Northern Hindostan, in the prov. of Ghurwal, at the confluence of the Alaknunda with the Pindar, 2,560 ft. above sea-level, and 30 m. NE of Sirinuggur, in N lat. 30° 14', E long. 79° 12'. It is one of the third in importance of the five prayagas or holy junctions of the Hindus. In 1809 it consisted of not more than 6 or 8 dwellings, in addition to the math or shrine of the deity.

CARNSORE POINT, a headland in the p. of Carne, co. Wexford, forming the SE extremity of the mainland of Ireland. It bears from St. David's head in Wales, NW 1 N 41 m. It was called by Ptolemy, Hieron, or the Sacred promontory; and anciently bore the names also of Salanga and Slievena-Domangaird.

CARNTOGHER, a mountain on the E border of Kenought, in co. Londonderry. It has an alt. of 1,521 ft. above sea-level; and forms one of the loftiest and most considerable masses in an extensive congeries of uplands. It is situated at nearly mid-distance between the channel of the Bann and that of the Roe.

CARNWATH, an extensive p. in the upper ward of Lanark, bounded by the Clyde on the S, and intersected by the Caledonian railway. Area 25,193 Scots acres, of which one-half is uncultivated. Pop. in 1801, 2,680; in 1841, 3,550, of whom the village of C. contained 796.
CARO, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Malestroit, 7 m. SSE of Ploermel. Pop. 1,601.
CAROCHA, or DUTCHMAN'S CAP, a small rocky island, in the bight of Biafra, to the SE of Prince's island, in N lat. 1° 36', E long. 7° 27'.

CAROGE, a township in the p. of Llanbister, Radnorshire. Pop. in 1841, 144. CAROL, LA TOUR-DE-CAROL, OF LA VALEE-DECAROL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Saillagouse. The valley, which runs SW, is about 9 m. in length, and is watered by the Fond-Vive. It contains a copper and a silver-mine, a copper wire-mill, and a hamlet of the same name, situated on the Carol, 33 m. WSW of Prades, and 17 m. from Saillagouse.

CAROLATH, a town of Prussia, in Silesia, in the regency and 45 m. NNW of Liegnitz, and 12 m. WNW of Glogau, on the r. bank of the Oder. Pop. 569. It gives its name to a principality comprising an area of about 36 m., and containing 2 towns, 21 villages, and 10,000 inhabitants.
CAROLEI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, district and 5 m. SW of Cosenza. Pop. 2,058. In the environs is a quarry of gun-flint.

CAROLINA, a town of Venezuela, in the depart. of Orinoco, on the Arui, 30 m. S of its junction with the Orinoco, and 50 m. SW of Angostura.-Also a town of New Grenada, in the prov. of Antioquia, in N lat. 6° 45', and at an alt. of 5,947 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,572.—Also a town of Brazil, cap. of the comarca of the same name, in the prov. and 500 m. NNE of Goyaz. The com. extends from the confluence of the N Manoel, Alvez, and Araguaia, with the Tocantins, to the Sommo on the S, and from the confines of the prov. of Minas Geraes to the Araguaia on the W.

CAROLINA (LA), a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 35 m. NNE of Jaen, on a ramification of the Sierra Morena, at an alt. of 1,800 ft. above sea-level. Pop., chiefly German, 1,739. It is of rectangular form, and is surrounded by walls; the streets are broad and straight, and the houses plain but uniform. It contains a church, some handsome fountains, and in the environs is a variety of fine promenades. Linen and common cloth are the chief articles of local manufacture. This town is one of the principal of the colonies established in 1767 and 1769 in the Sierra Morena by D. Paul Olavide.

CAROLINA (NORTH), one of the southern states of the North American confederacy; bounded on the N by Virginia; on the E by the Atlantic; on the S by South C. and Georgia; and on the W by Tennessee. It extends from 33° 50' to 36° 30' N lat., and from 75° 25' to 84° 30' W long. It is about 450 m. in length, by 185 m. in breadth; with an area of 50,000 sq. m. according to Goodrich; or 48,000 according to Haskel and Smith. Its superficial extent, therefore, is nearly equal to that of England and Wales. Physical features.] The W part of the state is an elevated table-land, and is traversed by the three easternmost chains of the Appalachian system,—the South-east mountain, the Blue ridge, and the Kittatinny mountains. The latter chain, under various local names,—as the Stone mountain, Iron mountain, Bald mountain, and Smoky mountain,-form the W boundary of the state. Black mountain, in Yancey co., reaches the height of 6,475 ft., being the loftiest summit E of the Mississippi. The Roan mountain is 6,038 ft. high; and there are others little inferior. Mount Ararat, or Pilot mountain, is a pyramidal peak 1,750 ft. in alt., in Stokes county. King's mountain is a hilly ridge extending from Lincoln county into York district in South Carolina.— The Roanoke and the Chowan, which rise in Virginia, empty themselves into Albemarle sound in this state. The latter is navigable for small vessels to water 30 m., and for boats to Tarboro', 90 m. Cape Fear river is the principal stream which has its whole course in this state. It rises in the N part, and traversing the state in a SE course of 280 m., falls into the Atlantic at Cape Fear. It is navigable for vessels of 11 ft. draft to Wilmington, and for boats to Fayetteville. The Yadkin traverses the W part of the state, from N to S, and passes into South C. under the name of the Great Pedee. The Catawba rises in the Blue ridge and flows S into South C. From the opposite slope of the mountains descend the head-streams of the Tennessee.—The coast— which is 300 m. in extent—is skirted by a range of low sandy islands, thrown up by the sea. They are long and narrow, and generally barren, and enclose several shallow bays and sounds. The largest is Pamlico sound, lying between the mainland and one of the above-mentioned islands. It is 86 m. in length along the coast, and from 10 to 20 m. broad. It communicates with the ocean by several narrow mouths, the most common of which for navigation is Ocracoke inlet. A little to the N is Albemarle | length, and 10 m. in breadth; and covers an extent sound, which extends 60 m. into the land, and is from 5 to 15 m. wide. It communicates with Pamlico sound, and with the sea, by several narrow and shallow inlets. The shores are low and marshy, and the navigation along the coast dangerous, on account of the shoals. Cape Lookout and Cape Fear indicate by their names the dread with which mariners approach them; but the most formidable is Cape Hatteras, the elbow of a triangular island, forming the seaward limit of Pamlico sound. Its shoals extend a great distance from the land, and render it one of the most dangerous headlands on the American coast. The sluggishness of the rivers as they approach the sea, and the sandy character of the coast, cause them to be extensively obstructed by bars at their mouth; and there are few good harbours.—The E part of the state, for a distance of about 60 m. from the sea, is a low plain covered with swamps, indented by numerous shallow inlets from the ocean, and traversed by sluggish streams, which the low and level surface allows to spread out into broad basins. To this maritime belt succeeds a fine undulating country, irrigated with fresh, running waters, and presenting a surface agreeably diversified with hills and valleys. The W part of the state isas we have said—an elevated table-land, rising to a general elevation of about 1,800 ft. above the level of the sea, independently of the mountain summits. Climate and soil.] In that small part of the state toward the W, which is mountainous, the climate is temperate and the air salubrious. This region is one of the most healthy in the country; and though the days in summer are hot, the nights are refreshed by cool breezes. In all the E parts the climate is unhealthy, and intermittent fevers are common in summer and autumn. The inhabitants have a pale, yellowish, and bilious complexion. According to observations made at the university of North C., in N lat. 35° 54′ 21″, W long. 79° 17′ 30″, from June 1847 to June 1848, the average height of the barometer at sunrise was 29.727 inches; at 9 A.M. 29.752 inches; at 9 P.M. 29.730 inches. The average temp. at sunrise and at 3 P.M., according to the detached therm .. was as follows: June 1847, 67°2 July, 69°2 August, 68°2 Sept 67°2 Sunrise. 78°2 3 P.M. The average temp. for the year at 9 A.M. was 59° 7'; at 9 P.M. 57° 8'. The hottest day occurred on 26th July; temp. at 9 A.M. 82°; at 3 P.M. 90°. The coldest day was 26th December; temp. at 9 A.M. 23°; at 9 P.M. 20°.—The number of rainy days was 73; of cloudy days, 229; of clear days, 37. The number of rainy days in the preceding year was 98; of cloudy days, 233; of clear days, 32. The winters are very mild. The wheat-harvest takes place in the beginning of June; the maize-harvest early in September.-In the level country generally, the soil is poor and sandy, with large swampy tracts. The banks of some of the rivers are tolerably fertile, and there are some glades of moist land, possessing a black, fruitful soil. W of the hilly country, the soil is good, and resembles that of the states further N. The Great Dismal swamp lies in the NE part of the State, and extends into Virginia. It is 30 m. in of 150,000 acres; the soil is marshy, and the whole tract is overgrown with pine, juniper, and cypress trees, with white and red oak in the drier parts. In the centre of it is a pond 15 m. in circuit. Many parts of this swamp are impervious to man, from the thickness of the woods and bushes. A canal, with a feeder 5 m. long, from Lake Drummond, is carried through it from Norfolk to Albemarle sound. Between Albemarle and Pamlico sound is another, called Alligator, or Little Dismal swamp, which also has a pond in the centre. This latter swamp has been partly drained by means of a canal, and the land rendered fit for the cultivation of rice. It is estimated that there are nearly 2,500,000 acres of swampy land within the state, capable of being drained at a trifling cost, and fitted for the culture of cotton, tobacco, rice, and maize. These swamps have a clay bottom, over which lies a thick stratum of vegetable compost. The drained lands are found to be exceedingly fertile. Geology.] The low country consists of deposits of sand and clay, similar to those of E Virginia, and belonging to the same geological age. These tertiary beds are not rich in minerals, but they comprise extensive deposits of shell-marl, fossiliferous limestones, copperas, and bog-iron ore. The line dividing the flat tertiary plain from the upper country, crosses the Neuse near Smithfield, and the Cape Fear near Averasboro', and is indicated by a ledge of micaceous rock. W of this line is a belt of micaslates, chlorite slates, gneiss, and granites. Among the minerals of this tract are the hematitic iron ores of Nash and Johnston, formerly wrought to advantage, and the plumbago or black-lead of Wake; soapstone and serpentine also occur. A belt of sandstone succeeds to this primary strip, extending SW from Granville quite across the state. Coal has been discovered in Orange and Chatham; the sandstone furnishes good freestones and grindstones; argillaceous iron ore—the usual accompaniment of coalmeasures-also occurs in this formation. Parallel with the freestone and coal-formation, on the W, lies the great slate-formation of North C. It extends across the state from NE to SW, covering more or less of the counties of Person, Orange, Chatham, Randolph, Montgomery, Cabarrus, Anson, and Mecklenburg. Its breadth is about 20 m. Within this district are found numerous beds of porphyry, soapstone, serpentine, greenstone, and hone or whetstone slate; manganese, specular oxide of iron, and brown hematites, also occur here. The novaculite, or honestone, is of a supeperior quality, and is preferred by mechanics to the best Turkey hones of the market. A second belt of primary rocks extends from the slate-formation nearly to the Blue ridge, and comprises the Gold regions of North C.; most of the gold has been procured from washings, and not from the veins in the quartzose rocks; it has been found in Guilford, Davidson, Randolph, Montgomery, Anson, Mecklenburg, Cabarrus, Rowan, Rutherford, and perhaps other counties; but we have no account of the actual quantity obtained. The famous mass which weighed 28 lbs., was found at Reed's mines in Cabarrus; other large lumps have been found, but they are rare. The gold from some of these mines is remarkable for its purity, and is sometimes kept in goose-quills, and exchanged by weight, constituting a part of the currency of the country; a considerable amount is also sent annually to the mint of the United States. There is a silver-mine in Davidson co., 10 m. SE of Lexington. Iron ore is also found in Rockingham, Stokes, Surrey, and Lincoln, and has been pretty extensively wrought; it is chiefly the magnetic oxide. Of the mineral resources of the more W counties we know little.—There are thermal saline springs in Buncombe co., called the Warm springs. The water is limpid, and gives out nitrogen gas. It contains muriates and sulphates of lime and magnesia. Productions.] A great part of the country is covered with forests of pitch-pine. In the plains of the low country, this tree is almost exclusively the natural growth of the soil. It much exceeds in height the pitch-pine of the northern states. The tar, turpentine, and lumber afforded by this valuable tree, constitute one-half the exports of the state. The moisture of the air in the swampy regions loads the trees with long spongy moss, which hangs in clusters from the limbs, and gives the forest a singular appearance. The mistletoe is often found upon the trees of the interior. The state also produces several valuable medicinal roots, as ginseng, Virginia and Seneca snakeroot, &c. The rich intervals are overgrown with canes, the leaves of which continue green throughout the winter, and afford good fodder for cattle.—The industry of this state is almost wholly agricultural. The great diversity of climate between the E lowlands and the W high country, causes a corresponding diversity in the produce; the former yielding cotton, rice, and indigo; and the latter, Indian corn, wheat, hemp, and tobacco. The cottoncrop does not exceed 30,000 bales. The mountainous districts are more generally appropriated to grazing, and large herds of cattle and horses are raised. Manufactures and commerce.] Manufactures can hardly be said to exist in this state, except in the shape of household industry; but during the last few years, several large cotton-mills have been erected and carried on successfully, and there are a few paper-mills and woollen-mills. The amount of home-made or family-manufactures within the state, in 1840, was 1,413,242 dollars. The cotton manufactories had 47,934 spindles, and employed 1,219 persons. There were 8 iron furnaces producing 968 tons of iron, and 43 forges. The lead smelted amounted to 10,000 lbs.; the gold to 255,618 dollars. The soap manufactured was 1,612,825 lbs.; tallow candles 148,546 lbs.; and the whole amount of capital employed in manufactures was 3,838,900 dollars. The dangers of the coast, and the want of good harbours, as well as the deficiency in channels of transportation, carry a great proportion of the trade of North C. to Virginia, South C., and Tennessee. The pine-forests, which cover nearly the whole of the eastern part of the state, yield not only much lumber for exportation, but also nearly all the resinous matter used in ship-building in this country. The resinous products are turpentine, scrapings, spirits-of-turpentine, rosin, tar, and pitch. Turpentine is merely the sap of the tree, obtained by making an incision in the bark. The turpentine flows out in drops, which fall into a box placed to receive The incisions are generally made about the it. middle of March; and the flow of turpentine usually ceases about the end of October. The boxes are emptied 5 or 6 times in the course of the year; and, on an average, 40 trees will yield a barrel of turpentine, and about a third of that amount of scrapings, or that part of the sap which becomes hard before it reaches the box. Oil or spirits-ofturpentine is made by distillation, during which process the oil comes over, and leaves a residuum called rosin. Tar is made by burning billets of pine under a heavy covering of turf or earth; a slow combustion without flame is thus caused, and the tar which exudes is collected by means of a trench, into a cavity dug in the ground for the purpose. The tar of the N of Europe is preferred in Europe to that of the United States, as it is much cleaner, better packed, and made from trees recently felled. Pitch is obtained from tar, by boiling it down to dryness. The lumber furnished by this state, includes ship-timber, shingles, staves, &c. The exports of the state, in 1840, amounted to 387,484 dollars; the imports, to 252,532 dollars. During the year ending June 30, 1847, the exports amounted to 284,919 dollars; the imports to 142,384 dollars. Canals and railroads. Little has hitherto been done in this state towards extending the facilities for transportation, although the most important productions are of a bulky nature, requiring cheap and easy modes of conveyance, and the character of the country offers many advantages for canalisation. The Dismal Swamp canal lies partly in this state, and the North-west canal, a branch of that work, is wholly within its limits; much of the trade of the NE counties takes this channel. The Clubfoot and Harlow canal is a short work extending from the Neuse to the harbour of Beaufort, and there are several short cuts round falls or shoals in the Roanoke, Tar, Cape Fear, and Yadkin.—The Portsmouth and Roanoke railroad, terminating at Weldon, the THE CONTRACTOR OF THE CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACTOR OF THE CONTRACTOR OF THE CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACTOR OF THE CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACTOR CONTRACT CONTRACT CONTRACT Petersburg and Roanoke railroad, terminating at and Raleigh railroad extends from the former place, by Wanesboro, and near Nashville and Enfield, through Halifax, to Weldon, 170 m. A line of steamboats from Wilmington to Charleston, 150 m., is connected with this route, which thus forms a link in the great chain of works from Maine to the gulf of Mexico. The Raleigh and Gaston railroad, from the capital to the Roanoke, is 85 m. long. Government, &c.] The legislature is styled the general assembly, and consists of a senate of 50, and a house-of-commons of 120 members. It meets biennially at Raleigh. The governor is chosen biennially by the people. Voters for senators must be freeholders, possessing at least 50 acres of land. The clergy are excluded from the legislature. The state sends 9 representatives to congress. No person who denies the being of a God, or the truth of the Christian religion, or the divine authority of the Old and New Testament, or who holds religious principles incompatible with the freedom or safety of the state, can hold any office or place of trust or profit, in the civil department, within the state. - The supreme court holds three sessions in each year: two in Raleigh, and one at Morgantown. There are 7 circuits of about 10 counties each. The Methodists number upwards of 20,000 communicants; the Baptists nearly the same number; and the Presbyterians, 11,000. The less numerous sects are the Episcopalians, who have 1 bishop and 20 ministers; the Lutherans, with 40 societies and 2,000 communicants; the Moravians, or United Brethren, with 4 congregations. There are also a few Roman Catholics, Friends, &c. The University of North C., at Chapel Hill, 27 m. WNW of Raleigh, was founded in 1791. It has 9 instructors, and 140 students. Its libraries have about 5,000 volumes. Davidson college, in Mecklen. burg co., was founded in 1837. It is regularly laid out, with some handsome buildings, but it is not a thriving place. Pop. 2,000. The state-house at this place was destroyed by fire, in 1831. This disaster was accompanied with the further and irreparable loss of Canova's admired statue of Washington; but a handsome capitol of granite has been erected in the place of the former building. -Newbern is the largest town in the state. It stands on the Neuse, 30 m. above Pamlico sound. Vessels from the sea ascend to this place, and it has a considerable trade in exporting timber, grain, and tar. This town has a theatre, and was once the seat of government. Pop. 4,000.—Fayetteville, on Cape Fear river, at the head of boat navigation, has a trade in grain, flour, tobacco, and naval stores. It contains a United States arsenal of construction. Pop. 3,000.—Wilmington, on Cape Fear river, 34 m. from the sea, is a commercial town, and the port for the trade of Fayetteville. In commerce, it is the first town in the state; but its harbour is not commodious for large vessels, being, like most others on this coast, barred with sand. The railroad to the Roanoke adds to its business and importance. Pop. 4,000.—Charlotte, in the interior, is a growing and prosperous town, with 2,000 inhabitants. Here is a branch of the United States mint, for the coinage of gold.—Salisbury, in the same region, has about 1,300 inhabitants. History.] This region was first visited by persons sent out by Sir Walter Raleigh, in 1584, who landed and traded with the natives at Pamlico and Albemarle sounds. The next year, a settlement was attempted on the island of Roanoke, in Albemarle sound, but after two or three years it was abandoned, and this part of the country was for a long time neglected. The first permanent settlements were made by the fugitives and seceders from Virginia, who, between 1640 and 1650, fled beyond the limits of that state to avoid religious persecution. A patent for the territory had been previously granted by Charles L, but no effectual use had been made of it.
